long.question: Define the term Gibbs free energy and derive the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation relating the change in Gibbs free energy $(\Delta G)$ and the temperature coefficient of $\Delta G$.
long.answer:
**OR, Define free-energy (G).** *[WBUT 2012(EVEN)]*

**1st Part:**
The portion of a total energy of a thermodynamic system which can be utilized for doing useful work i.e. available total energy is known as **Gibb's free energy** or **thermodynamic potential** of the system symbolically represented by $G$.

Thus,
$$\text{Enthalpy} = \text{Available enthalpy} + \text{unavailable enthalpy}$$
$$= \text{Gibb's free energy} + (\text{Temperature}\times\text{entropy})$$
$$H = G + TS$$
$$\text{or, } G = H - TS$$

**2nd Part:**
If a system changes reversibly from one state to another, the change in Gibbs free energy
$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S - S\Delta T$$
If changes occur at constant temperature
$$(\Delta G)_T = \Delta H - T\Delta S = \Delta H - T(S_f - S_i)$$
$$= \Delta H - T\left[-\left(\frac{\partial G_f}{\partial T}\right)_P - \left\{-\left(\frac{\partial G_i}{\partial T}\right)_P\right\}\right] = \Delta H + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left\{(G_f)_P - (G_i)_P\right\}\right]$$
$$(\Delta G)_P = \Delta H + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(\Delta G)\right]_P$$
where $(\partial G_f)_P$ and $(\partial G_i)_P$ are the small changes in Gibbs free energy for an infinitesimal change in temperature $\partial T$ at constant pressure for final and initial state. $S_f$ and $S_i$ are the entropies of the system at final and initial states.

long.question: What is entropy? What is its physical significance? Explain what you understand by internal energy of a system.
long.answer:
**1st Part:**
The heat change $dq$ and the temperature $T$ are thermodynamic quantities. Therefore, we can say that the system has a thermodynamic function whose change, measured by $\dfrac{dq}{T}$, is independent of the path of the transformation of the system. This function has been called entropy by Clausius and is denoted by the symbol $S$. Its change $dS$ is measured by the ratio of the reversible heat change and the temperature at which the heat change occurs i.e. $dS = \dfrac{dq_{rev}}{T}$.

**2nd Part:**
The state of equilibrium really means a state of maximum disorder or chaos. For example, if we put a quantity of yellowish green chlorine gas at a corner of the floor of the room, the gas though heavy would spread in all directions until it would be most chaotically distributed in the entire room and the equilibrium would be reached. Again if we arrange a number of red and black balls on a tray in a particular pattern and then shake the tray the balls would mix up in the most disorderly fashion. These are all natural processes and the reverse process is not possible in nature. Moreover, these processes which always have a tendency to attain the state of equilibrium are irreversible and thus leads to entropy increase. That means when the system approaches to equilibrium, the greater is the disorder and irreversibility and when equilibrium is reached, it has the maximum disorder. The entropy also goes on increasing and reaches its maximum value at the equilibrium state.

Thus, entropy is a measure of the disorder of the system or it may be called a measure of the mixedupness of the system.

**3rd Part:**
Internal energy of a thermodynamic system is the inherent energy possessed by the system within itself and it is denoted by the symbol $U$. Origin of internal energy comes from the molecular movement of the working substances of the system. Thus in a gas, the kinetic energy of the random moving molecules are responsible for the internal energy of the whole gas.

Example:
(i) zinc and copper sulphate in a Daniel Cell react producing electrical energy.
(ii) a liquid freezes into solid yielding heat energy.

long.question: Deduce Gibbs-Helmholtz equation in any of its standard forms.
long.answer:
**OR, Write short note on Gibbs-Helmholtz equation.** *[WBUT 2018(ODD)]*

The equations relating work function (A) with internal energy (U) of a thermodynamic system and Gibb's free energy (G) with total energy (H) of the system as well as respective change for a process are known as Gibbs Helmholtz equation.

Thus internal energy = Available internal energy + unavailable internal energy
$$= \text{Helmholtz free energy} + \text{Temperature}\times\text{entropy}$$
$$U = A + TS$$
$$\text{or } A = U - TS \qquad\ldots(1)$$

Whenever a system undergoes an infinitesimal reversible change, the corresponding change in work function
$$dA = dU - TdS - SdT = dU - dq - SdT = -PdV - SdT$$
At constant volume i.e. $dV = 0$
$$\therefore (dA)_V = -SdT$$
$$\text{or, } \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S$$
$$\text{or, } S = -\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V$$
Substituting the value of $S$ in equation (1) we get
$$A = U - T\left(-\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V\right)$$
$$A = U + T\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V \qquad\ldots(2)$$
This equation which co-relates work function (Helmholtz free energy) with internal energy is commonly known as Gibb's Helmholtz equation. Again if the system changes reversibly from one state to another state, the change in work function
$$\Delta A = \Delta U - T\Delta S - S\Delta T$$
At constant temperature
$$(\Delta A)_T = \Delta U - T(S_f - S_i)$$
$$= \Delta U - T\left[-\left(\frac{\partial A_f}{\partial T}\right)_V - \left\{-\left(\frac{\partial A_i}{\partial T}\right)_V\right\}\right] = \Delta U + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left\{(A_f)_V - (A_i)_V\right\}\right]$$
$$(\Delta A)_V = \Delta U + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(\Delta A)\right]_V \qquad\ldots(3)$$
where $\partial A_i$ and $\partial A_f$ are the changes in Helmholtz free energy at constant volume for an infinitesimal change in temperature for the initial and final state respectively. $S_f$ and $S_i$ are the entropies of the system at final and initial states.

Again we know
$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{A}{T}\right)\right]_V = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V + A\left(-\frac{1}{T^2}\right) = \frac{1}{T}(-S) + \left(-\frac{A}{T^2}\right) = -\left(\frac{A + TS}{T^2}\right) = -\frac{U}{T^2}$$
$$\therefore \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{A}{T}\right)\right]_V = -\frac{U}{T^2} \qquad\ldots(4)$$
Thus the above equation is another form of Gibbs Helmholtz equation.

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta A}{T}\right)\right]_V = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{A_f - A_i}{T}\right)\right]_V = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{A_f}{T}\right)\right]_V - \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{A_i}{T}\right)\right]_V$$
$$= -\left[\frac{U_f}{T^2}\right] - \left[-\frac{U_i}{T^2}\right] = -\left[\frac{U_f - U_i}{T^2}\right] = -\frac{\Delta U}{T^2}$$
$$\therefore \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta A}{T}\right)\right]_V = -\frac{\Delta U}{T^2} \qquad\ldots(5)$$

Similarly,
$$\text{Total energy} = \text{available total energy} + \text{unavailable total energy}$$
$$= \text{Gibb's free energy} + \text{Temperature}\times\text{entropy}$$
$$H = G + TS$$
$$\therefore G = H - TS \qquad\ldots(6)$$
$$\text{Again } G = (U + PV) - TS \qquad\ldots 6(a)$$
Whenever a system undergoes an infinitesimal change then corresponding change in Gibbs free energy is given by
$$dG = dU + PdV + VdP - TdS - SdT = dq + VdP - dq - SdT$$
$$\therefore dG = VdP - SdT$$
For an isobaric process i.e. $dP = 0$
$$(dG)_P = -SdT$$
$$\text{or, } \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$$
$$\text{or, } S = -\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P$$
Substituting this value of $S$ in equation (6) we get
$$G = H - T\left[-\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]$$
$$\text{or, } G = H + T\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P \qquad\ldots(7)$$
Again if a system changes reversibly from one state to another, the change in Gibbs free energy $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S - S\Delta T$. If changes occur at constant temperature
$$(\Delta G)_T = \Delta H - T\Delta S = \Delta H - T(S_f - S_i)$$
$$= \Delta H - T\left[-\left(\frac{\partial G_f}{\partial T}\right)_P - \left\{-\left(\frac{\partial G_i}{\partial T}\right)_P\right\}\right] = \Delta H + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left\{(G_f)_P - (G_i)_P\right\}\right]$$
$$(\Delta G)_T = \Delta H + T\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(\Delta G)\right]_P \qquad\ldots(8)$$
where $(\partial G_f)_P$ and $(\partial G_i)_P$ are the small changes in Gibbs free energy for an infinitesimal change in temperature $\partial T$ at constant pressure for final and initial state. $S_f$ and $S_i$ are the entropies of the system at final and initial states.

Again, we know
$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{G}{T}\right)\right]_P = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P + G\left(-\frac{1}{T^2}\right) = -\frac{S}{T} - \frac{G}{T^2} = -\left(\frac{G + TS}{T^2}\right) = -\frac{H}{T^2}$$
$$\therefore \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{G}{T}\right)\right]_P = -\frac{H}{T^2} \qquad\ldots(9)$$
Again
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right)_P = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{G_f - G_i}{T}\right)\right]_P = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{G_f}{T}\right)\right]_P - \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{G_i}{T}\right)\right]_P$$
$$= -\left(\frac{H_f}{T^2}\right) - \left(-\frac{H_i}{T^2}\right) = -\left[\frac{H_f - H_i}{T^2}\right] = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$$
$$\therefore \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right)\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2} \qquad\ldots(10)$$
Thus, the equations 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10 are known as Gibb's Helmholtz equation.

long.question: Using Carnot cycle, prove that the efficiency of a heat engine is always less than one.
long.answer:
No heat is rejected to the sink i.e. $Q' = 0$
$$W = Q - Q'$$
$$Q - Q' = Q\frac{T - T'}{T}$$
$$\text{or, } 1 - \frac{Q'}{Q} = 1 - \frac{T'}{T}$$
$$\text{or, } \frac{Q'}{Q} = \frac{T'}{T}$$
$$\text{or, } Q = Q'\frac{T}{T'}$$
$$\therefore W_{max} = Q\times\frac{T - T'}{T} = \frac{Q'}{T'}\times T\times\frac{T - T'}{T} = \frac{Q'}{T'}(T - T') = 0$$
i.e. no work can be obtained.

Hence to have work, heat must be supplied to the system from the source and moreover some heat must be rejected to the sink by the system i.e. heat must flow from source to sink.

Since to have work, some heat $(Q')$ must be rejected to the sink
$$\therefore Q - Q' \text{ must be } < Q$$
$$W < Q$$
$$\frac{Q - Q'}{Q} < 1$$
$$\therefore \eta < 1$$
i.e. 100% conversion is not feasible. Thus only a fractional quantity of supplied heat can be converted into work and this fraction is $\dfrac{T - T'}{T}$.

long.question: State the significance of Gibbs free energy.
long.answer:
**OR, Show that the decrease in Gibb's free energy at constant temperature and pressure is equal to the total work over and above the mechanical work.** *[WBUT 2014(EVEN), 2015(ODD)]*
**OR, What is the physical significance of free energy change $(\Delta G)$?** *[WBUT 2018(ODD)]*

If a system undergoes a reversible change under isothermal and isobaric conditions from state I to state II then,
$$(\Delta G)_{T,P} = (\Delta H)_{T,P} - T\Delta S \qquad [\because H = U + PV;\ (\Delta H)_{T,P} = \Delta U + P\Delta V]$$
$$= \Delta U + P\Delta V - T\Delta S \qquad [\because \Delta S = \frac{q_{rev}}{T};\ q_{rev} = T\Delta S]$$
$$= \Delta U - q_{rev} + P\Delta V$$
$$= -W + P\Delta V \qquad [\text{From 1st law } q = \Delta U + W;\ \Delta U - q = -W]$$
$$\text{or, } -(\Delta G)_{T,P} = W_{Total} - W_{mechanical}$$
$$= W_{nonmechanical}$$
(where $P\Delta V$ is the mechanical work involved in the system itself during transformation and $W$ denotes maximum total work output). $W_{nonmechanical}$ is the work received for any external use exclusive of the mechanical work e.g. electrical work.

long.question: What is a Carnot cycle? Obtain the expression for the efficiency of a reversible Carnot engine and starting from this expression state an appropriate statement of the second law of thermodynamics.
long.answer:
**1st Part:**
The brilliant French engineer Sadi Carnot in 1824 developed a cyclic process and explained clearly how and to what extent work is obtained from heat.

**2nd Part:**

**Requirement**

i) **System:** Perfect gas, enclosed in a cylinder, fitted with weightless, frictionless movable piston.

ii) **Surroundings:**
- a) High temperature thermostat (source) — supplier of heat
- b) Low temperature thermostat (sink) — acceptor of heat
- c) Thermally insulated jacket

iii) **Process:** The process is a cyclic process.

**Indicator Diagram:** *A P–V indicator diagram with corner points A $(P_1, V_1, T)$, B $(P_2, V_2, T)$, C $(P_3, V_3, T')$, D $(P_4, V_4, T')$; the enclosed area ABCD represents the work obtained. [diagram]*

At the start, the working substance is kept in the source till it attains the temperature of the source. Thus the temperature of the system becomes $T$. Let the corresponding pressure and volume be $P_1$ and $V_1$. This condition of the system is represented by the point A in the diagram. Then the system is subjected to the following operations, also represented in the P–V indicator diagram. The total enclosed area (ABCD) represents the work obtained.

**Operation I**
The cylinder having 1 mole of gas and the gas is allowed to expand isothermally and reversibly from the volume $V_1$ to the volume $V_2$.
The heat absorbed by the gas = $Q$ (say)
The work done by the gas $W = RT\ln\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$
Since the gas is ideal, we know, $Q = RT\ln\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$

**Operation II**
The cylinder is next taken out of the thermostat and kept in a thermally insulated enclosure. The gas is allowed to expand further from volume $V_2$ to volume $V_3$ adiabatically and reversibly until the temperature falls down to that of the sink $T'$.
The heat absorbed by the gas = Nil.
The work done by the gas $= C_v(T - T')$ ($C_v$ is the heat capacity of the gas).

**Operation III**
The cylinder is then placed in a thermostat at temperature $T'$ (sink); the gas is compressed isothermally and reversibly until the volume changes to $V_4$ from $V_3$.
The heat given out by gas = $Q'$ (say)
The work done by the gas $= RT'\ln\dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$
[Since $V_4$ is smaller than $V_3$, the work is negative which means work is done on the gas]

**Operation IV**
The cylinder is now thermally isolated as in operation II. The gas is then adiabatically and reversibly compressed to its initial volume $V_1$ and its original temperature $T$ is attained. The gas thus comes back to its initial state.
The heat absorbed by the gas = Nil
The work done by the gas $= C_v(T' - T)$

The net work done by the gas in the complete cycle,
$$W = RT\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} + C_v(T - T') + RT'\ln\frac{V_4}{V_3} + C_v(T' - T) = RT\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} + RT'\ln\frac{V_4}{V_3}$$
But considering the adiabatic changes in operation II and IV we get
$$TV_2^{\gamma-1} = T'V_3^{\gamma-1} \qquad\ldots(1)$$
$$TV_1^{\gamma-1} = T'V_4^{\gamma-1} \qquad\ldots(2)$$
Dividing $\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{V_3}{V_4}$
$$\therefore W = RT\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} - RT'\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1} = R(T - T')\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}$$
The efficiency of the process is given by the equation
$$\eta = \frac{W}{Q} = \frac{R(T - T')\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}}{RT\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}} = \frac{T - T'}{T} = 1 - \frac{T'}{T}$$
$$W = Q\times\frac{T - T'}{T}$$
This relation expresses the maximum amount of work obtained from the heat flowing from $T$ to $T'$. This is the mathematical form of the second law.

Since to have work, some heat $(Q')$ must be rejected to the sink
$$\therefore Q - Q' \text{ must be } < Q$$
$$W < Q$$
$$\frac{Q - Q'}{Q} < 1$$
$$\therefore \eta < 1$$
i.e. 100% conversion is not feasible. Thus only a fractional quantity of supplied heat can be converted into work and this fraction is $\dfrac{T - T'}{T}$. For these reasons, the Carnot cycle is regarded as the mathematical statement of 2nd Law of thermodynamics.

long.question: Prove that for criteria of spontaneity of $\Delta G$ should be negative.
long.answer:
We have
$$dS_{universe} = dS_{system} + dS_{surroundings}$$
$$= dS_{system} - \frac{dQ}{T} \quad [\text{If the surrounding supplies } dQ \text{ amount of heat to the system at temperature } T \text{ reversibly.}]$$
$$= dS_{system} - \frac{du + dw}{T} = dS_{system} - \frac{du}{T} - \frac{dw}{T}$$
$$= dS_{system} - \frac{du}{T} - \frac{PdV}{T} \quad [\text{If only mechanical work is involved}]$$
We have
$$A = U - TS$$
$$dA = dU - TdS - SdT$$
$$\text{or, } TdS - dU = -dA - SdT$$
$$\Rightarrow dS - \frac{dU}{T} = -\frac{dA}{T} - S\frac{dT}{T}$$
$$\Rightarrow dS_{universe} = -\frac{dA}{T} - \frac{SdT}{T} - \frac{PdV}{T}$$
Again we know
$$G = H - TS = U + PV - TS = A + PV$$
$$\therefore dG = dA + PdV + VdP$$
$$\Rightarrow -dA - PdV = VdP - dG$$
$$\Rightarrow -\frac{dA}{T} - \frac{PdV}{T} = \frac{VdP}{T} - \frac{dG}{T}$$
$$\Rightarrow dS_{universe} + \frac{SdT}{T} = \frac{VdP}{T} - \frac{dG}{T}$$
$$\Rightarrow dS_{universe} = -\frac{dG}{T} + \frac{VdP}{T} - \frac{SdT}{T}$$
Hence, $dS_{universe} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{T}\right)_{P,T}$.

But for a real process to occur, the change must be irreversible (i.e. spontaneous) since for irreversible process $dS_{universe} > 0$. Thus from above equation, $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{T}\right)_{P,T} < 0$ i.e. Gibbs free energy diminishes.

long.question: If 50 gm ice, initially at $-5°C$ is heated to water vapour finally at $127°C$, calculate entropy change for the entire process. (specific heat of ice and water vapour are 9.0 and 8 cal/mole respectively).
long.answer:
`[as printed: question states "427°C" in some impressions but the worked solution uses 127°C]`

Entropy change for the transformation of ice at $-5°C$ to ice at $0°C$
$$\Delta S_1 = mc\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 50\times 9\ln\frac{273}{268} = 8.028\ cal/K$$
Entropy change for the transformation of ice at $0°C$ to water at $0°C$
$$\Delta S_2 = \frac{18\times 80}{273} = \frac{1440}{273} = 5.275\ cal/K$$
Entropy change for the transformation of water at $0°C$ to water at $127°C$
$$\Delta S_3 = mc\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 50\times 8\ln\frac{400}{273} = 152.74\ cal/K$$
So total $\Delta S = \Delta S_1 + \Delta S_2 + \Delta S_3 = 8.028 + 5.275 + 152.74 = 166.043\ cal/K$.

long.question: Derive Maxwell's relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$.
long.answer:
According to the definition of work function
$$U = A + TS$$
$$A = U - TS$$
If the system undergoes infinitesimal change, then
$$dA = dU - TdS - SdT = dU - dq - SdT = -PdV - SdT \qquad [\text{as printed}]$$

**Corollary 1**
For isochoric process i.e. $dV = 0$
$$(dA)_V = -SdT$$
$$\text{or, } \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S$$
Second differentiation of $A$ with respect to $V$ at constant $T$ gives
$$\frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V\,\partial T} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

**Corollary 2**
For isothermal process i.e. $dT = 0$
$$(dA)_T = -PdV \qquad \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T = -P$$
Second differentiation of $A$ with respect to $T$ at constant $V$ gives, $\dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\,\partial V} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$.
Since $dA$ is a perfect differential, $\dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V\,\partial T} = \dfrac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\,\partial V}$
$$\therefore -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$
$$\text{or, } \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

long.question: Write down the applications of Gibbs Helmholtz equation.
long.answer:
(i) It is used to evaluate $\Delta H$ from e.m.f. of a cell.
(ii) It is used to derive Van't Hoff equation.
(iii) A knowledge of $\Delta G$ would enable us to ascertain $\Delta H$ of a process.

long.question: a) Discuss in brief the second law of thermodynamics. b) Write down the Maxwell's relations.
long.answer: Refer to Chapter at a Glance.

long.question: Explain the term chemical potential.
long.answer:
The partial molar Gibbs free energy is termed the chemical potential of species $i$ in the mixture. It is symbolically represented by $\mu$. Mathematically it is expressed as
$$\mu_i = \frac{\partial(nG)}{\partial n_i}\bigg|_{T,P,n_j}$$
$$\text{or, } \mu_i = \overline{G_i}$$

long.question: Show that, entropy of mixing of ideal gases $\Delta S_{mix} > 0$.
long.answer:
Two or more gases when brought in contact would immediately diffuse into one another and mix up irreversibly. Such a spontaneous irreversible process would of course lead to an entropy increase.

Let $n_A$ and $n_B$ gm moles of two gases A and B be mixed at a constant temperature $T$ and under pressure $P$. Before mixing, their entropy are
$$S_A = n_A\left[C_{P_A}\ln T - R\ln P + S_A^0\right]$$
$$S_B = n_B\left[C_{P_B}\ln T - R\ln P + S_B^0\right]$$
After mixing the total pressure $P$ remaining constant $(P)$, the partial pressures are $P_A$ and $P_B$; such that $P = P_A + P_B$. The entropies of the two components in the mixture are:
$$S_{A(m)} = n_A\left[C_{P_A}\ln T - R\ln P_A + S_A^0\right]$$
$$S_{B(m)} = n_B\left[C_{P_B}\ln T - R\ln P_B + S_B^0\right]$$
The entropy change due to mixing
$$\Delta S_m = S_{A(m)} + S_{B(m)} - S_A - S_B$$
$$= n_A C_{P_A}\ln T - n_A R\ln P_A + n_A S_A^0 + n_B C_{P_B}\ln T - n_B R\ln P_B + n_B S_B^0$$
$$\quad - n_A C_{P_A}\ln T + n_A R\ln P - n_A S_A^0 - n_B C_{P_B}\ln T + n_B R\ln P - n_B S_B^0$$
$$\Delta S_m = -n_A R\ln P_A - n_B R\ln P_B + n_A R\ln P + n_B R\ln P$$
$$= -n_A R\ln\frac{P_A}{P} - n_B R\ln\frac{P_B}{P}$$
The total amount of mixture $n = n_A + n_B$ moles.
Hence the mole fraction $x_A = \dfrac{n_A}{n}$ and $x_B = \dfrac{n_B}{n}$.
Now $\dfrac{P_A}{P} = x_A$ and $\dfrac{P_B}{P} = x_B$.
Hence the entropy change $\Delta S = -n_A R\ln x_A - n_B R\ln x_B$.
Dividing both side by $(n_A + n_B)$ we get
$$\frac{\Delta S}{(n_A + n_B)} = \overline{\Delta S} = -\frac{n_A}{n_A + n_B}R\ln x_A - \frac{n_B}{n_A + n_B}R\ln x_B$$
$$\therefore \text{Entropy change per mole} = \overline{\Delta S} = -x_A R\ln x_A - x_B R\ln x_B$$
In general mixing for any number of gases
$$\Delta S_m = -\sum x_i R\ln x_i = -R\sum x_i\ln x_i$$
The fraction $x_i$ is less than unity in all cases, the logarithm is negative and thus $\Delta S_m$ is always positive $(\Delta S_{mix} > 0)$.

long.question: Write short note on Features of Ellingham diagram.
long.answer:
Graphical representation of $\Delta G^0$ vs. $T$ plot in the formation of oxides of elements is known as Ellingham Diagram. Ellingham diagram of oxides has the following features:

(i) $\Delta G^0$ vs. $T$ curve for metal to metal oxide shows positive slope because the free energy change decreases with temperature.
(ii) Curves in the Ellingham diagram for the formation of metallic oxide are straight line with positive slope, unless there is a change of state.
(iii) With increase in temperature a point is reached where the curve crosses the $\Delta G = 0$ line. Below this temperature, the free energy of formation of the metal oxide is negative and the oxide is stable. Above this temperature, the free energy of formation of the metal oxide is positive and the oxide is unstable. Thus the oxide decomposes to metal and oxygen.
(iv) Stability of the metal oxides decreases with increase in temperature.
(v) In different extraction processes of metal, one metal is used to reduce the oxides of other metal. It can be explained from Ellingham diagram that a metal will reduce the oxide of other metal which lies above it in the diagram.

long.question: What is the physical significance of Helmholtz free energy?
long.answer: Refer to Chapter at a Glance.

long.question: If the process is completely adiabatic then how much work is obtained from a reversible Carnot engine?
long.answer: If the process be completely adiabatic i.e. no heat is absorbed. $\therefore Q = 0$ then $W_{max} = 0$ $\therefore$ No work can be obtained.

long.question: For a reaction $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ both are positive. Under what condition will the reaction be spontaneous?
long.answer:
For a reaction $\Delta H = (-)ve$ and $\Delta S = (+)ve$ `[as printed]`
$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$
At high temperature $T\Delta S > \Delta H$, then $\Delta G = (-)ve$ and the reaction will be spontaneous.

long.question: Compare the thermodynamic efficiencies to be expected (a) When an engine operates between 1000°K and 300°K, (b) When an engine operates between 1000°K and 600°K and waste heat is passed to another engine working between 600°K and 300°K.
long.answer:
Case (a) Efficiency $\eta_1 = \dfrac{1000 - 300}{1000} = 0.7$

Case (b) $\dfrac{W_1}{Q_1} = \dfrac{1000 - 600}{1000} = 0.4$ $\therefore W_1 = 0.4Q_1$

So waste heat $Q_2 = Q_1 - 0.4Q_1 = 0.6Q_1$ then the heat is passed to another which does work $W_2$ and
$$\frac{W_2}{0.6Q_1} = \frac{600 - 300}{600} = 0.5$$
So, $W_2 = 0.3Q_1$
Total work $= W_1 + W_2 = 0.4Q_1 + 0.3Q_1 = 0.7Q_1$
So total efficiency $= \dfrac{0.7Q_1}{Q_1} = 0.7$ i.e. same with the efficiency of the engine working between 1000°K and 300°K. `[as printed: text writes "1000°C and 800°K"]`

long.question: Calculate $\Delta S$ per litre of solution when pure $N_2$, $H_2$ and $NH_3$ gases are mixed to form a solution having the final composition 15% $N_2$; 55% $H_2$ and 30% $NH_3$ (all at S.T.P.).
long.answer:
This is a case of free mixing and we know that for free mixing
$$\Delta S = -\sum X_i R\ln X_i/\text{mole. Here } X_i = \text{Mole fraction.}$$
In the given problem $X_{N_2} = 0.15$; $X_{H_2} = 0.55$ and $X_{NH_3} = 0.30$
So $\Delta S = -0.15\times 1.987\ln 0.15 - 0.55\times 1.987\ln 0.55 - 0.30\times 1.987\ln 0.30$
or, $\Delta S = (0.565 + 0.653 + 0.718)\ Cal/mole$
or, $\Delta S = 1.936\ cal/mole$
As they are at S.T.P. 1 mole $\equiv$ 22.4 litre
So, $\Delta S/\text{litre} = 0.086\ Cal/deg/litre$.

long.question: A mole of steam is condensed at 100°C, the water is cooled to 0°C and frozen to ice. What is the difference in entropies of the steam and ice? The heats of vaporization and fusion are 540 cal.gm⁻¹ and 80 cal.gm⁻¹ respectively. Use the average heat capacity of liquid water as 1 cal.gm⁻¹.degree⁻¹.
long.answer:
Entropy change during condensation of steam
$$\Delta S_1 = -\frac{18\times 540}{373}\ Cal/° = -26.06\ Cal/°$$
Entropy change during cooling of water from 100°C to 0°C
$$\Delta S_2 = 18\times 1\ln\frac{273}{373}\ Cal/° = -5.62\ Cal/°$$
Entropy change during freezing of water at 0°C
$$\Delta S_3 = -\frac{18\times 80}{273}\ cal/° = -5.27\ cal/°$$
So that entropy change $= -26.06 - 5.62 - 5.27 = -36.95\ Cal/°$
Hence difference in entropy between steam and ice $= 36.95\ cal/°$.

long.question: 5 gm-moles of water initially at 27°C are converted to a final state of vapour at 227°C, the conversion being effected under 1 atmospheric pressure. Assuming the vapour to behave ideally, compute the total change in entropy.
long.answer:
[heat capacity of water = 1 cal/gm; heat capacity of water vapour = 0.40 cal/gm, latent heat of vaporization of water = 540 Cal/gm] *[MODEL QUESTION]*

Entropy change during heating 5 gm moles (i.e. 90 gm) water from 27°C (i.e. 300°A) to 100°C (i.e. 373°A) is
$$\Delta S_1 = 90\times 1\ln\frac{373}{300} = 19.60\ Cal/°$$
Entropy change during vaporization of 5 moles water at 373°A is
$$\Delta S_2 = \frac{90\times 540}{373} = 130.29\ Cal/°$$
And entropy change during heating 5 moles vapour from 100°C (i.e. 373°A) to 227°C (i.e. 500°A) is
$$\Delta S_3 = 90\times 0.40\ln\frac{500}{373} = 10.55\ Cal/°$$
So total change $\Delta S = \Delta S_1 + \Delta S_2 + \Delta S_3 = (19.6 + 130.29 + 10.55)\ \text{e.u.} = 160.44\ Cal/°$.

long.question: Five moles of each A and B are mixed together at one atm. pressure and at 300 K. Calculate $\Delta G$, $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ of mixing assuming that A and B form ideal mixture.
long.answer:
$n_A = n_B = 5$ moles.
Thus mole fraction of A, $x_A = \dfrac{n_A}{n_A + n_B} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.5$
and mole fraction of B, $x_B = \dfrac{n_B}{n_A + n_B} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.5$
Thus
$$\Delta S_{mix} = -x_A R\ln x_A - x_B R\ln x_B = -0.5\times 8.314\ln 0.5 - 0.5\times 8.314\ln 0.5$$
$$= 2.881 + 2.881 = 5.762\ J\,mol^{-1}K^{-1}$$
$\Delta H_{mix}$ for ideal gas $= 0$
Thus
$$\Delta G_{mix} = \Delta H_{mix} - T\Delta S_{mix} = 0 - 300\times 5.762 = -1728.6\ J/mol/K = -1.728\ KJ\,mol^{-1}K^{-1}$$

long.question: Calculate the free energy change which occurs when one mole of an ideal gas expands reversibly and isothermally at 300K from an initial volume of 5 litre to 50 litres.
long.answer:
In isothermal reversible process, the change in Gibbs free energy
$$(\Delta G)_T = nRT\ln\frac{V_1}{V_2} = 1\times 1.987\times 300\ln\frac{5}{50}$$
$$= -1.987\times 300\times 2.302 = -1372.22\ Cal$$

long.question: Show that $dq$ is not a perfect differential but $\dfrac{dq}{T}$ is a perfect differential.
long.answer:
Suppose one mole of a perfect gas undergoes a reversible expansion from volume $V_1$ to volume $V_2$ when the temperature changes from $T_1$ to $T_2$. Then from the first law
$$dq = dU + dw$$
$$\text{Or, } dq = dU + PdV$$
$$\text{or, } \int_I^{II} dq = \int_I^{II} dU + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} PdV = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_v dT + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} RTd\ln V = C_v(T_2 - T_1) + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} RTd\ln V$$
The integral on the right hand side $\int RTd\ln V$ can not be evaluated unless we know the relation between $T$ and $V$. If $T$ be constant (i.e. isothermal change) the integral will have one value, and if $T$ changes during the process, the integral will have different values.

Thus, the magnitude of $\int_I^{II} dq$ depends on the way the expansion is carried out. Hence, $dq$ is not a perfect differential.

Now dividing the equation by $T$, we have
$$\int_I^{II}\frac{dq}{T} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_v\frac{dT}{T} + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} Rd\ln V = C_v\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}$$
That is, the $\int\dfrac{dq}{T}$ for a given change from state I to state II can be evaluated without any reference to the path of transformation. Thus, $\int\dfrac{dq}{T}$ is independent of the way the change is carried out. Hence, $\dfrac{dq}{T}$ is a perfect differential.

long.question: Prove that entropy change $(dS)$ is independent of the path of the transformation.
long.answer:
*A P–V diagram is shown with AB and CD as isothermal stages (heat $dq_1$ at $T_1$, heat $dq_2$ at $T_2$) and BC, DA as adiabatic stages. [diagram]*

Here AB and CD are isothermal stages at temperature $T_1$ and $T_2$; BC and DA adiabatic stages. Let $dq_1$ be the heat supplied to the working system at $T_1$ and $dq_2$ be the heat rejected by it to the sink at $T_2$. All steps are reversible. We know in a Carnot cycle,
$$\frac{dq_1 - dq_2}{dq_1} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1}$$
$$\text{or, } \frac{dq_1}{T_1} = \frac{dq_2}{T_2} \qquad (dq_2 \text{ is negative as it is rejected by the system})$$
Now let us confine our attention only to the change of the system from the point A to the point C and attempt to find out the ratio of the heat change to the temperature at which the thermal change occurs.

We can proceed from A to C either along ABC or along ADC.
Along the path ABC, we have the isothermal AB, when heat change $dq_1$ takes place at $T_1$, followed by adiabatic change BC having no heat change.
Hence, $\dfrac{\text{Heat change}}{\text{Temperature}} = \dfrac{dq_1}{T_1} + 0 = \dfrac{dq_1}{T_1}$

Along the path ADC, the adiabatic AD involves no thermal change; but along the isothermal DC, heat absorbed would be $dq_2$ at temperature $T_2$.
Hence, $\dfrac{\text{Heat change}}{\text{Temperature}} = 0 + \dfrac{dq_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{dq_2}{T_2}$

Since from reversible Carnot cycle, we have, $\dfrac{dq_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{dq_2}{T_2}$

Thus the ratio of heat change to the temperature i.e. $\dfrac{dq}{T}$ is the same whatever path may be followed by the system in its reversible change. Hence $\dfrac{dq_{rev}}{T}$ is regarded as a perfect differential.

The entropy change $(dS)$ is measured by the ratio of the reversible heat change and the temperature at which the heat change occurs i.e. $dS = \dfrac{dq_{rev}}{T}$.

Since, $\dfrac{dq_{rev}}{T}$ is independent of the path of the transformation, thus, entropy change $(dS)$ is also independent of the path of the transformation.

long.question: Prove that $(\Delta G)_T = (\Delta A)_T$.
long.answer:
For ideal gas $\int dG_T = \int\dfrac{nRT}{P}dP$
$$G_T = nRT\ln P + n\overline{G_0}$$
Again integrating within limits we get,
$$\int_{G_1}^{G_2}(dG)_T = \int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{nRT}{P}dP$$
$$(G_2 - G_1)_T = nRT\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1} = nRT\ln\frac{V_1}{V_2}$$
$$= -W_{max} = (\Delta A)_T$$
Hence, $(\Delta G)_T = (\Delta A)_T$.

---

*End of Chapter 5: Thermodynamics - II.*

concepts.title: Electrochemical Cell
concepts.body: Electrochemical cell is an arrangement in which two metal rods (called electrodes), either dipped in the solution of same electrolyte or placed separately in two different electrolyte solutions. They are connected by a salt bridge. When the electrodes are externally connected by a metallic wire, current flow from one electrode to the other and inside the cell chemical changes occur at the surface of the electrodes. The electrode where reduction occurs is called the cathode or positive electrode and where oxidation takes place is called the anode or negative electrode.

concepts.title: Types of electrochemical cell
concepts.body:
Electrochemical cell may be of two types
- Electrolytic cell
- Galvanic cell

concepts.title: Electrolytic cell
concepts.body:
It is a device in which the electrical energy is converted into chemical energy through chemical reaction.

**Example:** Electrolysis of acidified water by using platinum electrode is an example of electrolytic cell.

concepts.title: Galvanic cell
concepts.body:
A galvanic cell is a device in which the free energy of a chemical process is converted into electrical energy. Usually such cells consist of two electrodes partially immersed in one or more suitable electrolytes.

When the electrodes are externally connected by a metallic wire, a current flow from one electrode to another and inside the cell chemical changes occur at the surface of the electrodes.

concepts.title: Reaction in Galvanic cell
concepts.body:
During the production of current, zinc atoms pass into solution as $\text{Zn}^{++}$ ions leaving behind two electrons each in the metal electrode. On the other hand copper ions from the solution are deposited on the copper electrode by accepting two electrons each from the metal. So there is an excess of electrons in the zinc electrode and less electron in the copper electrode.

$$\text{Zn(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + 2e \quad \text{(Oxidation)} \qquad \ldots(1)$$
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu(s)} \quad \text{(Reduction)} \qquad \ldots(2)$$

When the circuit is closed, the electrons from zinc flow to copper through the wire. Therefore the current is produced.

By adding (1) and (2) we get the net cell reaction
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + 2e$$
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$\overline{\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{++} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + \text{Cu}}$$

concepts.title: Representation of a Galvanic cell
concepts.body:
- An electrode of the Galvanic cell is represented by writing the symbol for the metal/non-metal followed by that of the solution or the ion in which the metal/non-metal is immersed.
- The two species i.e. ions or molecules present in the same phase are separated by a comma.
- The phase boundary between two miscible liquids is represented either by a single dotted vertical line or double solid vertical line which indicates the salt bridge between the two electrolytic solutions used in the electrodes.
- It is the usual convention to write the negative electrode of the cell on the left and the positive electrode to the right in representing a cell.
- The concentration of the substances involved are represented within first bracket followed symbols/formulas of the ions/solutions/solids/gases etc.

The Galvanic Cell is represented as
$$\overset{(-)}{\text{Zn}} \mid \text{Zn}^{++} (\text{Conc } C_1) \parallel \text{Cu}^{++} (\text{Conc } C_2) \mid \overset{(+)}{\text{Cu}}$$

concepts.title: Salt bridge
concepts.body: Salt bridge is usually an inverted U-tube filled with concentrated solution of inert electrolytes. An inert electrolyte is one whose ions neither involved in any electrochemical change nor do they react chemically with the electrolytes in two half-cells. Generally salts like $\text{KCl}$, $\text{KNO}_3$, and $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ etc. are used. For the preparation of salt bridge, gelatin or agar-agar is dissolved in a hot concentrated aqueous solution of an inert electrolyte and solution thus formed is filled in the U-tube. On cooling the solution set in the form of a gel in the U-tube. The ends of the U-tube are plugged with cotton wool as to minimize diffusion effects.

concepts.title: Reversible cell
concepts.body: In reversible cell the chemical reaction occurring in the cell may proceed in either direction depending upon direction of flow of current. When the circuit of a cell is closed a reaction occurs in the cell and a current is obtained. If we apply a driving force from an external EMF, so as to cause a current to flow in the opposite direction the chemical action is exactly and fully reversed. When these requirements are satisfied, the cell is reversible.

concepts.title: Irreversible cell
concepts.body: When the chemical process cannot be exactly reversed by the application of external EMF the cell is irreversible.

concepts.title: Standard Electrode Potential
concepts.body: Standard electrode potential is the electrode potential of all the species involved in an electrode is in their standard states of unit activity with reference to standard hydrogen electrode. The standard states may be considered as: (a) 1.0 M concentration of each ion in the solution (b) 1 atmospheric pressure for each gas and (c) 25°C or 298K temperature. The standard electrode potential of an electrode is represented by $E^0$.

concepts.title: Significance of the sign of the standard electrode potential
concepts.body:
- If standard electrode potential ($E^0$) of an electrode is positive then reduction occurs in the electrode and the electrode is designated as cathode or (+) ve electrode.
- If standard electrode potential ($E^0$) of an electrode is negative then oxidation occurs in the electrode and the electrode is designated as anode or (−) ve electrode.

concepts.title: Assignment of (−) ve and (+) ve electrode in a cell
concepts.body:
- If the standard reduction potential of one electrode in a cell is (+) ve and another electrode in the cell is (−) ve, then the (+) ve electrode is considered as cathode (reduction occurs) and (−) ve electrode is considered as anode (oxidation occurs).
- If the standard reduction potential of both the electrodes are (+) ve, then the electrode which has more (+) ve value will be considered as (+) ve electrode or cathode (reduction occurs) and the electrode which has less (+) ve value will be considered as (−) ve electrode or anode (oxidation occurs).
- If the standard reduction potential of both the electrodes are (−) ve, then the electrode which has more (−) ve value will be considered as (−) ve electrode or anode (oxidation occurs) and the electrode which has less (−) ve value will be considered as (+) ve electrode or cathode (reduction occurs).

concepts.title: Standard EMF a Cell
concepts.body:
Standard EMF is the EMF of a cell when all the substances present in the cell or participate in the cell reaction are in their standard states of unit activity. The standard EMF of the cell can be computed from the standard electrode potentials of the individual electrodes.

EMF of a Cell can be computed by determining the reduction potential of the positive electrode or cathode and the reduction potential of the negative electrode or anode in the following way.

- The EMF of the cell is obtained by subtracting the reduction potential of the negative electrode from the reduction potential of the positive electrode.

Hence
$$E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_{+} - (E_{\text{reduction}})_{-}$$

concepts.title: Nernst equation
concepts.body:
Nernst equation is used to determine the EMF of a cell. Let us consider a reaction in a Galvanic Cell where $n_1$ moles of reactant A reacts with $n_2$ moles of another reactant B producing $n_3$ moles of D and $n_4$ moles of E at a given temperature T and activity $a$.

$$n_1 A + n_2 B \rightleftharpoons n_3 D + n_4 E$$

Then according to Nernst equation,
$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.05913}{n} \log \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}}$$

concepts.title: Hydrogen Half Cell
concepts.body:
| Representation | Reaction | Expression of potential |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Pt, H}_2(g, P\text{-atm}) \mid \text{H}^+$ | $\text{H}^+ + e \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(g)\ (P\text{-atm.})$ | $E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{H}^+,\text{H}_2} - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln P_{\text{H}_2} + \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln a_{\text{H}^+}$ ; $\quad E = -\dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln P_{\text{H}_2} - \dfrac{RT \times 2.303}{F}\,\text{pH}$ |

concepts.title: Calomel Half Cell
concepts.body:
| Representation | Reaction | Expression of potential |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Hg}(l) - \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) \mid \text{Cl}^-$ (Saturated with $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$) | $2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e$ | $E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2, \text{Hg}} - \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}$ |

concepts.title: Quinhydrone Electrode
concepts.body:
| Representation | Reaction | Expression of potential |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Pt} \mid$ Solution saturated with Quinhydrone, $\text{H}^+$ | $\text{H}_2\text{Q} \rightleftharpoons \text{Q} + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e$ | $E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Q}, \text{H}_2\text{Q}} + \dfrac{RT}{F} \times 2.303\,\text{pH}$ ; $\quad = E^0_{\text{Q}, \text{H}_2\text{Q}} - \dfrac{RT}{F} \times 2.303\,\text{pH}$ |

**Advantages of quinhydrone electrode**
- It is simple and easier to set up.
- It does not require constant attention.
- Equilibrium is quickly attained, hence it is not so time consuming.
- A small quantity of solution is needed.
- Air need not be eliminated.
- It can be used in presence of a) heavy ions b) unsaturated substances.

**Limitations quinhydrone electrode**
- In alkaline medium hydroquinone undergoes arial oxidation to form phenoxide ion. Hence it cannot be used in alkaline medium. So it is not of such a general use as $\text{H}_2$ - electrode.
- It is found that when, pH < 7.8 the half cell potential is positive; pH > 7.8 the half cell potential is negative; pH = 7.8 the half cell potential is zero.
- It cannot be used if any other red-ox system is present in experimental solution.
- It cannot be used in solution of very high activity of ions.

---

long.question: Discuss the physicochemical principle involved in the measurement of pH of an aqueous solution by using a hydrogen electrode.
long.answer:
**OR, Derive the pH of an electrochemical cell with the help of Nernst equation.** *[WBUT 2012(EVEN)]*

**Hydrogen Half Cell**

**Construction**

A solution containing $\text{H}^+$ is taken in a pyrex vessel provided with lid. Though the lid a pt-wire is introduced which carry a pt-foil coated with pt-black and kept immersed in solution, pure $\text{H}_2$ gas at pressure P is continuously bubbled so that the pt-black and the solution are saturated and equilibrium is reached. Guard tube is fitted to ensure that external atmospheric gases do not diffuse in the system. Temperature is maintained constant by placing it in a thermostat.

> *[diagram]* A pyrex vessel containing the $\text{H}^+$ solution, with $\text{H}_2$ gas (P atm) bubbled in through a tube, a platinized Pt foil immersed in the solution, and a guard tube.

**Representation**
$$\text{Pt, H}_2(g, P\text{-atm}) \mid \text{H}^+$$

**Reaction**

The actual reaction for hydrogen half cell is
$$\text{H}^+ + e \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2(g)\ (P\text{-atm.}) \quad \text{(Reduction)}$$

**Expression of potential**

From Nernst equation we get, the reduction potential
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{H}^+ \to \text{H}_2} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{H}_2(g)}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}^+}}$$
$$= E^0_{\text{H}^+ \to \text{H}_2} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{H}_2}^{1/2} + \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{H}^+}$$
$$= E^0_{\text{H}^+ \to \text{H}_2} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln a_{\text{H}_2} + \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{H}^+}$$
$$= E^0_{\text{H}^+ \to \text{H}_2} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln P_{\text{H}_2} + \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{H}^+}$$

($P_{\text{H}_2} \to$ Pressure at which $\text{H}_2$ gas is passed)

**Discussion**

1) The hydrogen half cell potential depends on (a) temperature (b) activity of $\text{H}^+$ ion (c) pressure at which $\text{H}_2$ gas is passed.

2) If $\text{H}_2$ gas at one atmosphere pressure is kept in constant with a solution containing $\text{H}^+$ ion of unit activity then it is called standard or normal hydrogen electrode half cell (SHE). Here $E_{\text{H}_2} = E^0$ $\because a_{\text{H}^+} = 1$ and $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$.

Then $E^0$ is called standard hydrogen electrode potential (SHEP). By convention this potential $E^0$ is arbitrarily taken a zero at all temperature. Thus for
$$\text{Pt, } \underset{p=1\,\text{atm}}{\text{H}_2(g)} \mid \text{H}^+(a_{\text{H}^+} = 1), \quad E = E_{\text{H}_2} = 0 \text{ volt.}$$

3) Thus
$$E = -\frac{RT}{2F} \ln P_{\text{H}_2} - \frac{RT \times 2.303}{F}\,\text{pH}$$

So pH of the solution can be ascertained by measuring the HEP.

long.question: What is reference electrode? Explain the working principle of one reference electrode.
long.answer:
**1st Part:**

**Reference Electrode:** An electrode whose electrode potential is accurately known or whose electrode potential has been arbitrarily fixed.

Example: Calomel electrode, hydrogen electrode.

**2nd Part:**

**Calomel Electrode:**

**Construction**

The electrode consists of a glass vessel A to which one side arm B is attached for making electrical contact and another arm C for insertion of any desired solution into the bottom of A. A Pt-wire is sealed into the bottom of A over which are placed in turn a layer of specially purified mercury and paste of mercury and calomel and then the appropriate KCl solution saturated with calomel was filled into the cell.

> *[diagram]* A glass vessel with a platinum wire sealed at the bottom, layered (from bottom up) with mercury, a paste of $\text{Hg} + \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$, and KCl solution saturated with $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$; side arm B for electrical contact and arm C for solution insertion.

**Representation**
$$\text{Hg}(l) - \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) \mid \text{Cl}^- \text{ (Saturated with Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2)$$

**Reaction**
$$2\text{Hg} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{Hg}^+ + 2e$$
$$2\text{Hg}^+ + 2\text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2$$
$$\overline{2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e}$$

**Expression of Potential**

Let the actual process occurring at the electrode be reduction, then the electrode reaction will be $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s) + 2e \to 2\text{Hg}(l) + 2\text{Cl}^-$.

Then the electrode potential will be given by
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \to \text{Hg}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Hg}(l)}^2 \times a_{\text{Cl}^-}^2}{a_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(s)}}$$
$$= E^0_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \to \text{Hg}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}^2 = E^0_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \to \text{Hg}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}$$

**Discussion**

(1) Electrode potential depends upon the activity of KCl solution used, usually three standard calomel electrode are used in the laboratory with reduction potential.

| Concentration of KCl | Saturated | 1.0 N | 0.1 N |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrode Potential (reduction) | 0.2415 V | 0.2800 V | 0.3338 V |

(2) If $a_{\text{Cl}^-} = 1$ then $E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \to \text{Hg}} \Rightarrow$ standard calomel potential. Because of the experimental difficulties in the preparation of $\text{H}_2$ – Half Cell, calomel half cell is extensively used as reference half cell. As it is standard and not primary standard $\text{H}_2$ half cell it is called secondary standard half cell.

(3) It can be easily prepared and can attain reversibility readily. Hence it is extensively used over $\text{H}_2$ electrode.

long.question: Write down the electrode reaction and over all reaction of the following cells:
long.answer:
**i)** $\text{Zn(s)} \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) \parallel \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \mid \text{Cu(s)}$
**ii)** $\text{Pt(s)} \mid \text{H}_2(g) \mid \text{H}^+(aq) \parallel \text{Ag}^+(aq) \mid \text{Ag(s)}$

**i)**
$$\overset{(-)}{\text{Zn(s)}} \mid \text{Zn}^{++}(aq) \parallel \text{Cu}^{++}(aq) \mid \overset{(+)}{\text{Cu(s)}}$$
By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get cell reaction
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + 2e$$
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$\overline{\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{++} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + \text{Cu}}$$

**ii)**
$$\overset{(-)}{\text{Pt(s)} \mid \text{H}_2(g) \mid \text{H}^+(aq)} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{Ag}^+(aq) \mid \text{Ag(s)}}$$
By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Ag}^+ + e \longrightarrow \text{Ag} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get cell reaction
$$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e$$
$$2\text{Ag}^+ + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{Ag}$$
$$\overline{\text{H}_2 + 2\text{Ag}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{Ag}}$$

long.question: What is calomel electrode? How can we measure the pH of a solution using calomel electrode?
long.answer:
**Calomel electrode:** Here mercury with one of its sparingly soluble salt mercurous chloride in contact with a solution of potassium chloride saturated with mercurous chloride will form calomel electrode.

**pH measurement:** The experimental solution whose pH is to be measured is taken in a beaker. A pinch of quinhydrone (which is equimolar mixture of compound quinone $\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$ and Hydroquinone $\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{OH})_2$ are held together by hydrogen bonding. Consequently there is decrease in co-association) is added to it, with stirring to ensure saturation. Then a bright Pt wire (or foil) is suspended in the solution. In solution quinhydrone breaks up to equimolecular proportion of quinone and hydroquinone such that
$$a_{\text{Q}} = a_{\text{H}_2\text{Q}}$$

Thus we have both quinine (Q) and reduction product of hydroquinone ($\text{H}_2\text{Q}$) present together in solution, containing $\text{H}^+$ ions. Consequently the following oxidation and reduction leading to occur,
$$\text{i.e. } \text{H}_2\text{Q} \rightleftharpoons \text{Q} + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e$$

> *[diagram]* Structural formulae showing hydroquinone (benzene ring with two OH groups) $\rightleftharpoons$ quinone (ring with two =O groups) $+\ 2\text{H}^+ + 2e$.

This indeed a red-ox system, so the immersed Pt wire records a potential. Evidently a red-ox half cell known as quinhydrone half cell is set up, $\text{Pt} \mid$ quinhydrone, $\text{H}^+$ of experimental solution. Thus $\text{H}_2\text{Q}$ electrode is then coupled with a saturated calomel electrode, so as to be a complete cell. The liquid junction potential is eliminated by means of a suitable salt bridge. In this combination $\text{H}_2\text{Q}$ acts as the $\oplus$ ve electrode. Hence the complete cell is represented as:
$$\text{Hg} \mid \text{HgCl}_2(s) \mid \text{saturated KCl solution} \parallel \text{Q, H}_2\text{Q (Saturated) experimental solution} \mid \text{Pt}$$

As reduction takes place at cathode hence,
$$\text{Q} + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e \Rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{Q}$$

Reduction potential $E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Q} \to \text{H}_2\text{Q}} - \dfrac{RT}{F} \times 2.303\,\text{pH}$. As the calomel electrode acts as the (−) ve electrode and consequently involved oxidation types of reduction, its potential will be $E_{\text{calomel (ox)}}$, hence the net EMF,
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E_+) - (E_-) = E^0_{\text{Q} \to \text{H}_2\text{Q}} - 0.059\,\text{pH} - E_{\text{calomel (reduction)}}$$
$$\text{pH} = \frac{-E_{\text{cell}} + E^0_{\text{Q} \to \text{H}_2\text{Q}} - E_{\text{calomel (reduction)}}}{0.059}$$

$E_{\text{cell}}$ is measured experimentally while $E_{\text{calomel}}$ and $E^0_{\text{Q} \to \text{H}_2\text{Q}}$ can be obtained from standard reduction potential table. Hence pH can be calculated.

long.question: Calculate the EMF of the following cell:
long.answer:
$\text{Ni} \mid \text{Ni}^{2+}(1M) \parallel \text{Pb}^{2+}(1M) \mid \text{Pb}$ at 25°C. Given standard electrode potentials of Ni and Pb are −0.24V and −0.13V respectively at 25°C.

The reduction potential value of $\text{Ni} \mid \text{Ni}^{2+} = -0.24\text{V}$
The reduction potential value of $\text{Pb} \mid \text{Pb}^{2+} = -0.13\text{V}$

The electrode reaction (reduction) of the right hand electrode is $\text{Pb}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Pb}$
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Pb}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Pb}}}{a_{\text{Pb}^{2+}}} = -0.13 + \frac{0.0592}{2} \log a_{\text{Pb}^{2+}} \quad [a_{\text{Pb}} = 1]$$
$$= -0.13 + 0.0296 \log 1 = -0.13\text{V}$$

The electrode reaction (reduction) of the left hand electrode is $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Ni}$
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Ni}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Ni}}}{a_{\text{Ni}^{2+}}} = -0.24 + \frac{0.0592}{2} \log a_{\text{Ni}^{2+}}$$
$$= -0.24 + 0.0296 \log 1 = -0.24\text{V}$$

Hence $E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = (-0.13 + 0.24)\text{ V} = 0.11\text{ V}$

long.question: For the following cell, write down the cell reaction and calculate the e.m.f. at 25°C.
long.answer:
$\text{Ag} \mid \text{Ag}^+(a = 0.1) \parallel \text{Zn}^{2+}(a = 0.1) \mid \text{Zn}$
Given $E^0_{\text{Ag}^+ \mid \text{Ag}} = 0.799\text{ V}$ and $E^0_{\text{Zn}^{2+} \mid \text{Zn}} = -0.763\text{ V}$

The standard reduction potential of $\text{Zn}^{2+} \mid \text{Zn}$ electrode is $= -0.763$ V
The standard reduction potential of $\text{Ag}^+ \mid \text{Ag}$ electrode is $+ 0.799$ V

Hence $\text{Zn}^{2+} \mid \text{Zn}$ is anode or negative electrode and $\text{Ag}^+ \mid \text{Ag}$ is cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Ag}^+ + e \longrightarrow \text{Ag} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the cell reaction
$$(1) \times 1 \Rightarrow \text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e$$
$$(2) \times 2 \Rightarrow 2\text{Ag}^+ + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{Ag}$$
$$\overline{\text{Zn} + 2\text{Ag}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{Ag}}$$

The electrode reaction reduction in the LHE is $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Zn}$
$$(E_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}})_- = E^0_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} - \frac{0.05912}{2} \log \frac{a_{\text{Zn}}}{a_{\text{Zn}^{2+}}} = -0.763 - 0.0295 \log \frac{1}{0.1} \quad [\text{Since } a_{\text{Zn}} = 1]$$
$$= -0.763 - 0.0295 = -0.7925\text{ V}$$

The electrode reaction reduction in the RHE is $\text{Ag}^+ + e \longrightarrow \text{Ag}$
$$(E_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}})_+ = E^0_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}} - \frac{0.05912}{1} \log \frac{a_{\text{Ag}}}{a_{\text{Ag}^+}} = 0.799 - 0.05912 \log \frac{1}{0.1} \quad [\text{Since } a_{\text{Ag}} = 1]$$
$$= 0.799 - 0.05912 = 0.73988\text{ V}$$

Thus EMF of the cell is
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}})_+ - (E_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}})_- = 0.7399 - (-0.7925) = 1.5324\text{ V.}$$

long.question: a) The $E^0$ values of Cu, $\text{Cu}^{+2}$ is −0.34 V. Calculate the concentration of $\text{Cu}^{+2}$ when the electrode potential is zero.
long.answer:
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu};$$
$$E_{\text{Cu}^{++}/\text{Cu}} = E^0_{\text{Cu}^{++}/\text{Cu}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Cu}}}{a_{\text{Cu}^{++}}}$$
$$\Rightarrow 0 = -0.34 + \frac{0.05913}{2} \ln a_{\text{Cu}^{++}}$$
$$\Rightarrow \ln a_{\text{Cu}^{++}} = \frac{0.34}{0.02956} = 11.5021$$
$$\Rightarrow a_{\text{Cu}^{++}} = 3.159 \quad \text{[as printed]}$$

**b) Where do you use glass electrode to measure the pH of a solution?** *[WBUT 2016(ODD)]*

The glass electrode is used to measure the pH of foods, cosmetics, analysis of soil acidity etc.

**c) What type of storage cell is in your mobile phone?** *[WBUT 2016(ODD)]*

In mobile phone lithium-ion battery is used as storage cell.

long.question: Derive the relation of EMF of cell with $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$.
long.answer:
**Relation between EMF of a cell and $\Delta G$:**

If $E$ be the EMF of a cell supplying a quantity of electricity $Q$, then the electrical energy is $EQ$.

If each equivalent of reactant involved is associated one faraday, $F$ of electricity. Then for $n$ equivalent of reaction occurs, electrical energy produced $= nFE$.

Again, according to Helmholtz, "the decrease in free energy of the process is equal to the electrical energy obtained from a Galvanic cell".

So, $-\Delta G = nFE$
or, $\Delta G = -nFE$

So, as '$n$' and '$F$' is known, measuring EMF ($E$) of the cell we can measure $\Delta G$.

**Relation between EMF of a cell and $\Delta H$:**

From Gibbs-Helmholtz equation we know that
$$\Delta G = \Delta H + T\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G)}{\partial T}\right]_P$$
or, $-nFE = \Delta H + T(-nF)\left(\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$
$$\therefore \Delta H = -nFE + TnF\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$
$$\therefore \Delta H = nF\left[T\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P - E\right]$$
where, $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial T}$ = Temperature coefficient of the cell.
