long.question: Write short notes on the following:
long.answer:
**a) Nernst's electrode potential** *[WBUT 2007, 2014(EVEN), 2016(ODD), 2017(ODD)]*
**b) Calomel electrode** *[WBUT 2009, 2011(ODD), 2015(EVEN), 2016(ODD)]*
**c) Reference electrode** *[WBUT 2010(ODD), 2014(ODD)]*

**a) Nernst's electrode potential:**

Let us consider a reaction in a Galvanic Cell where, $n_1$ moles of reactant A reacts with $n_2$ moles of another reactant B producing $n_3$ moles of D and $n_4$ moles of E at a given temperature T and activity $a$.
$$n_1 A + n_2 B \rightleftharpoons n_3 D + n_4 E$$

The change in the thermodynamic potential of the system due to the chemical reaction
$$\Delta G_T = \sum G_{\text{resultant}} - \sum G_{\text{reactant}} \quad [\because G = \text{free energy of the respective species}]$$
$$= (G_D + G_E) - (G_A + G_B)$$
$$= (n_3 \mu_D + n_4 \mu_E) - (n_1 \mu_A + n_2 \mu_B) \quad [\because G_i = n_i \mu_i,\ n_i = \text{number of moles of } i\text{th species}]$$
$$= \{n_3(\mu_D^0 + RT \ln a_D) + n_4(\mu_E^0 + RT \ln a_E)\} - \{n_1(\mu_A^0 + RT \ln a_A) + n_2(\mu_B^0 + RT \ln a_B)\}$$
$$= [(n_3 \mu_D^0 + n_4 \mu_E^0) - (n_1 \mu_A^0 + n_2 \mu_B^0)] + [(RT \ln a_D^{n_3} + RT \ln a_E^{n_4}) - (RT \ln a_A^{n_1} + RT \ln a_B^{n_2})]$$
$$= \Delta G^0 + RT \ln \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}} \qquad \ldots(1)$$
$[\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i,$ where $a_i$ = activity of the $i$th component$]$

The decrease in the free energy of the process is equal to the electrical energy obtained from a Galvanic Cell i.e.
$$-\Delta G = EQ = EnF$$

[Where, E is the EMF of the cell supplying a quantity of electricity Q. Such that the electrical energy $= EQ$. $F$ is the faraday of the electricity associated with one equivalent of reactants. $n$ = the no. of equivalents participating in the reaction]

Thus $\Delta G = -nFE$ $\qquad \ldots(2)$
So $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$ $\quad (E^0 = $ EMF of the standard cell$)$

Substituting (2) in equation (1) we get
$$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}}$$
$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}}$$

This relation is known as Nernst equation
$$\therefore E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96500 \times n} \log \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}} = E^0 - \frac{0.05913}{n} \log \frac{a_D^{\,n_3} \times a_E^{\,n_4}}{a_A^{\,n_1} \times a_B^{\,n_2}}$$

This form of Nernst equation is used in calculation.

**b) Calomel electrode:** *Refer to Question No. 2.2.*

**c) Reference electrode:** *Refer to Question No. 2.2.*

long.question: What is the difference between electrochemical cell and electrolytic cell?
long.answer:
| Electrochemical cell | Electrolytic cell |
|---|---|
| (i) Here the chemical energy is converted to electrical energy | (i) Here, the electrical energy from an external source is used to bring about the chemical change. |
| (ii) Here spontaneous redox reaction occurs | (ii) Here the redox reaction is non-spontaneous and takes place only when electrical energy is supplied from outside. |
| (iii) Here anode is assigned as negative and cathode is assigned as positive electrode. | (iii) Here anode is connected to the positive terminal and cathode to the positive terminal of battery. `[as printed]` |
| (iv) Two electrodes are set up in two different vessels. | (iv) Two electrodes are set up in the same vessel. |
| Example: Dry cell, lead storage battery. | Example: Changing lead storage. |

long.question: What is the role of the salt bridge in a voltaic cell?
long.answer: The salt bridge provides a mechanism by which ions not directly involved in the red-ox reaction can migrate into the anode and cathode compartments to maintain charge neutrality of the solution.

long.question: Why do we use $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ or KCl for preparing salt bridge?
long.answer: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ or KCl is used for the preparation of salt bridge because the transport number of $\text{NH}_4^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ or $\text{K}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ are nearly equal to 0.5 each. So there is no net transfer of these ions during the operation of a cell.

long.question: What is electrochemical cell? How can you increase the value of reduction potential of an electrode?
long.answer:
A device used to convert the chemical energy produced in a red-ox reaction into electrical energy is known as electrochemical cell.

The value of reduction potential of an electrode may be increased by increasing the concentration of metal ions in contact with metal electrode or increasing the temperature.

long.question: Why do electrochemical cells stop working after some time?
long.answer: An electrochemical cell produces electrical energy at the cost of red-ox reaction. When the red-ox reaction is completed, the cell stops working, since it is now incapable of undergoing red-ox reaction any more.

long.question: Write the electrochemical theory of rusting.
long.answer:
Following reactions take place during rusting.
$$\text{Anode: } \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{++} + 2e$$
$$\text{Cathode: } 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

The $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ ions formed at anode are oxidized by $\text{O}_2$ to $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ ions which then react with $\text{OH}^-$ ions to form hydrated ferric oxide, i.e., rust.
$$2\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^-$$
$$2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 6\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_3 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

long.question: Why is hydrogen electrode not generally used in pH measurement?
long.answer:
Hydrogen electrode is not generally used in pH measurement because
(i) It is difficult to set up
(ii) It can not be used in red-ox systems
(iii) It can not be used in presence of $\text{Hg, As, S, Fe}^{3+}, \text{MnO}_4^-, \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ etc.

long.question: Write down the individual electrode reactions and also the total cell reaction for each of the following cells
long.answer:
**(a)** $\overset{(-)}{\text{Pb} \mid \text{PbSO}_4(S), \text{SO}_4^{--}} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{Cu}^{++} \mid \text{Cu}}$
**(b)** $\overset{(-)}{\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{++}} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{H}^+ \mid \text{H}_2 (\text{Pt})}$

**a)** The reduction potential value of $\text{Pb} \mid \text{PbSO}_4(S), \text{SO}_4^{--} = -0.3505\text{V}$
The reduction potential value of $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++} = +0.337$

Hence $\text{Pb} \mid \text{PbSO}_4(S), \text{SO}_4^{--}$ is anode or negative electrode and $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++}$ is cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the all reaction
$$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e$$
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$\overline{\text{Cu}^{++} + \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{Cu} + \text{PbSO}_4}$$

**b)** The reduction potential value of $\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{++} = -0.761\text{ V}$
The reduction potential value of $\text{H}_2 \mid \text{H}^+ = 0.000\text{V}$

Hence $\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{++}$ is anode or negative electrode and $\text{H}_2 \mid \text{H}^+$ is cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get cell reaction
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e$$
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e \longrightarrow \text{H}_2$$
$$\overline{\text{Zn} + 2\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{++} + \text{H}_2}$$

long.question: Give the cell reactions of the following cells and calculate their standard e.m.f at 25°C using the standard reduction potential value
long.answer:
**(a)** $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(S) \mid \text{Cl}^- \parallel \text{Cu}^{++} \mid \text{Cu}$
**(b)** $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Fe}^{++} \text{-Fe}^{+++} \parallel \text{Cl}^- \mid \text{AgCl(S)} \mid \text{Ag}$

**a)** $\overset{(-)}{\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(S) \mid \text{Cl}^-} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{Cu}^{++} \mid \text{Cu}}$

The standard reduction potential value of $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(S) \mid \text{Cl}^- = +0.268\text{V}$
The standard reduction potential value of $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++} = +0.337\text{V}$

The reduction potential value of both the electrode is positive but the value of $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++}$ is greater than the value of $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(S) \mid \text{Cl}^-$.

So $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2(S) \mid \text{Cl}^-$ is the anode or negative electrode and $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++}$ is called cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e$$
$$\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$\overline{2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^- + \text{Cu}^{++} \longrightarrow \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Cu}}$$

Hence $E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = (0.337 - 0.268)\text{V} = 0.069\text{V}$

**b)** $\overset{(+)}{\text{Pt} \mid \text{Fe}^{++}\text{-Fe}^{+++}} \parallel \overset{(-)}{\text{Cl}^- \mid \text{AgCl(S)} \mid \text{Ag}}$

The standard reduction potential value of $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Fe}^{++}\text{-Fe}^{+++} = +0.771\text{V}$
The standard reduction potential value of $\text{Ag} - \text{AgCl(S)} \mid \text{Cl}^- = +0.2223\text{V}$

The reduction potential value of both the electrode is positive but the value of $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Fe}^{++}\text{-Fe}^{+++}$ is greater than that value of $\text{Ag} \mid \text{AgCl(S)} \mid \text{Cl}^-$. So $\text{Ag} \mid \text{AgCl(S)} \mid \text{Cl}^-$ is anode or negative electrode and $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Fe}^{++}\text{-Fe}^{+++}$ is called cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs at anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{AgCl} + e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occur at cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Fe}^{+++} + e \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{++} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{AgCl} + e$$
$$\text{Fe}^{+++} + e \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{++}$$
$$\overline{\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- + \text{Fe}^{+++} \longrightarrow \text{AgCl} + \text{Fe}^{++}}$$

Hence $E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = (0.771 - 0.223)\text{V} = 0.5487\text{V}$ `[as printed: text reads 0.223 here though the value above is 0.2223]`

long.question: Compute the cell potentials of the following
long.answer:
$\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++}(a = 0.01) \parallel \text{Zn}^{++}(a = 0.1) \mid \text{Zn}$

$$\overset{(+)}{\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++}(a = 0.01)} \parallel \overset{(-)}{\text{Zn}^{++}(a = 0.1) \mid \text{Zn}}$$

The reduction potential value of $\text{Cu} \mid \text{Cu}^{++} = +0.337\text{V}$
The reduction potential value of $\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{++} = -0.761\text{V}$

The electrode reaction (reduction) of the left hand electrode is $\text{Cu}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Cu}^{++} \to \text{Cu}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Cu}}}{a_{\text{Cu}^{++}}} = 0.337 + \frac{0.0592}{2} \log a_{\text{Cu}^{++}} \quad [\because a_{\text{Cu}} = 1]$$
$$= 0.337 + 0.0296 \log 10^{-2} = 0.337 - 0.0296 \times 2 = 0.337 - 0.0592 = 0.2778 \approx 0.278\text{V}$$

The electrode reaction (reduction) of the right hand electrode is $\text{Zn}^{++} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Zn}$
$$E_{\text{red}} = E^0_{\text{Zn}^{++} \to \text{Zn}} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Zn}}}{a_{\text{Zn}^{++}}} = E^0_{\text{Zn}^{++} \to \text{Zn}} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln a_{\text{Zn}^{++}} \quad [a_{\text{Zn}} = 1]$$
$$= -0.761 + 0.0592 \log a_{\text{Zn}^{++}} = -0.761 + 0.0296 \log 10^{-1}$$
$$= -0.761 - 0.0296 = -0.7906\text{ V}$$

Hence $E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = (0.278 + 0.7906)\text{ V} = 1.0686\text{ V.}$

long.question: Construct electrochemical cell for the following reaction and then find out the standard free energy change from their e.m.f
long.answer:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Br}^- \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Br}_2 + \text{Cl}^-$$

$$\underset{\text{Oxidation}}{\overset{\text{Reduction}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Br}^- \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Br}_2 + \text{Cl}^-}}$$

Here $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{Cl}_2 + e \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^-$. In this reaction chlorine (Oxidation number = 0) is reduced to $\text{Cl}^-$ (oxidation number = − 1), hence the positive half will be the $\text{Cl}_2 - \text{Cl}^-$ half cell i.e. $(\text{Pt})\,\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Cl}^-$ is cathode or positive electrode.

Again $\text{Br}^- \longrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Br}_2 + e$. Here bromide (oxidation number = − 1) is oxidized to bromine (oxidation number = 0) hence the negative half cell is $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Br}_2 \mid \text{Br}^-$. The electrochemical cell where the given reaction occurs is represented as
$$\overset{(-)}{\text{Pt, Br}_2 \mid \text{Br}^-} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{Cl}^- \mid \text{Cl}_2 (\text{Pt})}$$

The reduction potential value of $\text{Br}_2 \mid \text{Br}^- = 1.0652\text{ V}$
The reduction potential value of $\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Cl}^- = 1.359\text{ V}$

The e.m.f. of the cell
$$E_{\text{Cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = (1.359 - 1.0652)\text{ V} = 0.2983\text{ V}$$

Standard free energy change
$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 = -1 \times 96500 \times 0.2983 \text{ Joule} = -28351.7 \text{ Joules} = -2.83517 \times 10^4 \text{ Joules.}$$

long.question: For the cell $\text{H}_2 \mid \text{HCl} \mid \text{AgCl, Ag}$ $E^0 = 0.222\text{V}$. If the measured e.m.f is 0.385 V, What is the pH of HCl solution? $[P_{\text{H}_2} = 1\text{ atm}]$
long.answer:
The reaction in the cell $(\text{pt})\,\text{H}_2 (1\text{ atm}) \mid \text{HCl} \mid \text{AgCl, Ag}$ is
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 + \text{AgCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag} + \text{HCl}$$
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{\text{HCl}} \quad [P_{\text{H}_2} = 1\text{ atm}]$$
or, $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{2RT}{F} \ln a_{\pm}$ $\quad [a_{\pm} = $ mean ionic activity$]$
or, $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - 0.118 \log a_{\pm}$ $\quad [$at 25°C$]$
or, $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - 0.118\,P_{\text{H}}$ $\quad [-\log a_{\pm} = -\log a_{\text{H}^+} = P_{\text{H}}]$
or, $0.385 - 0.222 + 0.118\,P_{\text{H}}$
or, $P_{\text{H}} = 1.38$ `[as printed]`

long.question: From the standard reduction potentials
long.answer:
**i)** $\text{Ce}^{3+} + 3e \to \text{Ce}$ $\quad E^0 = -2.48\text{V}$
**ii)** $\text{Ce}^{4+} + e \to \text{Ce}^{3+}$ $\quad E^0 = 1.61\text{V}$
Calculate the reduction potential for one half cell $\text{Pt} \mid \text{Ce, Ce}^{4+}$.

$$\text{Ce}^{3+} + 3e \to \text{Ce}$$
$$\text{Ce}^{4+} + e \to \text{Ce}^{3+}$$
$$\overline{\text{Ce}^{4+} + 4e \to \text{Ce}}$$
$$E^0_{\text{Ce}^{3+}/\text{Ce}} = -2.48\text{V} \quad (-\text{ve Electrode})$$
$$E^0_{\text{Ce}^{4+}/\text{Ce}^{3+}} = 1.61\text{V} \quad (+\text{ve Electrode})$$
Thus $E^0_{\text{Ce}^{4+}/\text{Ce}} = E^0_{\text{Ce}^{4+}/\text{Ce}^{3+}} - E^0_{\text{Ce}^{3+}/\text{Ce}}$
$$= 1.61 - (-2.48) = 4.09\text{ V.}$$

long.question: What is the EMF of the following cell at 25°C?
long.answer:
$\text{Zn(s)} \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}(0.01M) \parallel \text{Ag}^+(0.1M) \mid \text{Ag(s)}$
Given that the standard reduction potentials of $\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}$ and $\text{Ag} \mid \text{Ag}^+$ half cells are −0.76V and +0.80V respectively.

The standard reduction potential of $\text{Zn}^{2+} \mid \text{Zn}$ electrode is $= -0.76\text{V}$
The standard reduction potential of $\text{Ag}^+ \mid \text{Ag}$ electrode is $+ 0.80\text{V}$

Hence $\text{Zn}^{2+} \mid \text{Zn}$ is anode or negative electrode and $\text{Ag}^+ \mid \text{Ag}$ is cathode or positive electrode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Ag}^+ + e \longrightarrow \text{Ag} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the cell reaction
$$(1) \times 1 \Rightarrow \text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e$$
$$(2) \times 2 \Rightarrow 2\text{Ag}^+ + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{Ag}$$
$$\overline{\text{Zn} + 2\text{Ag}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{Ag}}$$

The electrode reaction reduction in the LHE is $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Zn}$
$$(E_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}})_- = E^0_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} - \frac{0.05912}{2} \log \frac{a_{\text{Zn}}}{a_{\text{Zn}^{2+}}} = -0.76 - 0.0295 \log \frac{1}{0.01} \quad [\text{Since } a_{\text{Zn}} = 1]$$
$$= -0.76 - 0.059 = -0.819\text{V}$$

The electrode reaction reduction in the RHE is $\text{Ag}^+ + e \longrightarrow \text{Ag}$
$$(E_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}})_+ = E^0_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}} - \frac{0.05912}{1} \log \frac{a_{\text{Ag}}}{a_{\text{Ag}^+}} = 0.80 - 0.05912 \log \frac{1}{0.1} \quad [\text{Since } a_{\text{Ag}} = 1]$$
$$= 0.80 - 0.05912 = 0.741\text{ V}$$

Thus EMF of the cell is
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}})_+ - (E_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}})_- = 0.741 - (-0.819) = 1.56\text{ V.}$$

long.question: The EMF of the cell is 0.445V
long.answer:
$$\overset{(-)}{\text{Pt, H}_2(1\text{ atm}) \mid \text{H}^+ (\text{Unknown})} \parallel \overset{(+)}{\text{KCl(Satd)} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Hg.}}$$
Calculate the pH of the solution $E^0_{\text{KCl(Satd)}} = 0.2415\text{V}$.

$$\text{H}^+ + e \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2$$
$$E_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} = E^0_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{a_{\text{H}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}^+}} = 0 - \frac{0.05912}{1} \log \frac{1}{a_{\text{H}^+}} = -0.05912\,\text{pH}$$
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E_{\text{calomel}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_-$$
or, $0.445 = 0.2415 + 0.05912\,\text{pH}$
or, $0.05912\,\text{pH} = 0.2035$
or, $\text{pH} = \dfrac{0.2035}{0.05912} = 3.44$

long.question: The standard reduction potential for $\text{Sn}^{2+} + 2e \to \text{Sn}$ and $\text{Sn}^{4+} + 4e \to \text{Sn}$ are −0.136 V and 1.05 V respectively. Calculate $E^0$ and $\Delta G^0$ for $\text{Sn}^{4+} + 2e \to \text{Sn}^{2+}$.
long.answer:
From standard reduction potential value it is clear that $\text{Sn}^{2+} \mid \text{Sn}$ is negative electrode or anode and $\text{Sn}^{4+} \mid \text{Sn}$ is positive electrode or cathode.

By convention oxidation occurs is anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Sn} \longrightarrow \text{Sn}^{2+} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Sn}^{4+} + 4e \longrightarrow \text{Sn} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$\text{Sn} \longrightarrow \text{Sn}^{2+} + 2e$$
$$\text{Sn}^{4+} + 4e \longrightarrow \text{Sn}$$
$$\overline{\text{Sn}^{4+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Sn}^{2+}}$$

$E^0_{\text{Sn}^{2+}/\text{Sn}} = -0.136$ V
$$G^0 = -nFE^0 = -2 \times 96500 \times (-0.136) = 26248 \text{ J}$$
$E^0_{\text{Sn}^{4+}/\text{Sn}} = 1.05$ V
$$G^0 = -nFE^0 = -4 \times 96500 \times 1.05 = -405300$$
$$\Delta G^0 = (26248 - 405300)\text{J} = -379052\text{J}$$
$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 \Rightarrow E^0 = -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$
Thus
$$E^0_{\text{Sn}^{4+}/\text{Sn}^{2+}} = -\frac{(-379052)}{2 \times 96500} = \frac{379052}{193000} = 1.964\text{ V}$$

long.question: Write down the overall cell reaction for
long.answer:
$$\overset{(+)}{\text{Pt, Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Cl}^-} \parallel \overset{(-)}{\text{H}^+ \mid \text{H}_2, \text{Pt}}$$

The reduction potential value of $\text{H}^+ \mid \text{H}_2$ electrode is 0 volt and $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Cl}^-$ is 0.2415 V.

Hence $\text{H}^+ \mid \text{H}_2$ is negative electrode or anode and $\text{Hg} \mid \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \mid \text{Cl}^-$ is positive electrode or cathode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^- \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e$$
$$\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^-$$
$$\overline{\text{H}_2 + \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{Hg} + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{Cl}^-}$$

long.question: Calculate the equilibrium constant K for the following reaction at 298 K
long.answer:
$$3\text{Sn}^{4+} + 2\text{Cr} \longrightarrow 3\text{Sn}^{2+} + 2\text{Cr}^{3+}$$
Given $E^0_{\text{Sn}^{4+}/\text{Sn}^{2+}} = 0.15\text{V}$, and $E^0_{\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}} = -0.74\text{V}$

Here $\text{Sn}^{4+}/\text{Sn}^{2+}$ is positive electrode or cathode and $\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}$ is negative electrode or anode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Cr} \longrightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Sn}^{4+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Sn}^{2+} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$(1) \times 2 \Rightarrow 2\text{Cr} \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6e$$
$$(2) \times 3 \Rightarrow 3\text{Sn}^{4+} + 6e \longrightarrow 3\text{Sn}^{2+}$$
$$\overline{2\text{Cr} + 3\text{Sn}^{4+} \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{Sn}^{2+}}$$

Thus $E^0_{\text{cell}} = (E^0_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E^0_{\text{reduction}})_- = 0.15 - (-0.74) = 0.89\text{ V.}$
$$E_0 = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K$$
$$\therefore \ln K = \frac{0.89 \times 6 \times 96500}{8.314 \times 298} = 207.989 \quad \text{or } K = 2.132 \times 10^{90}.$$

long.question: Calculate the maximum work that can be accomplished by the operation of the following cell at 25°C
long.answer:
$\text{Ni(s)} \mid \text{Ni}^{2+}(0.01M) \parallel \text{Cu}^{2+}(0.1M) \mid \text{Cu(s)}$
Given $E^0_{\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}} = -0.15\text{V}$ and $E^0_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\text{V}$.

Here $\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}$ is positive electrode or cathode and $\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}$ is negative electrode or anode.

By convention oxidation occurs in anode or negative electrode
$$\text{Ni} \longrightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e \qquad \ldots(1)$$
and reduction occurs in cathode or positive electrode
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu} \qquad \ldots(2)$$
By adding (1) and (2) we get the total cell reaction
$$\text{Ni} \longrightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e$$
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$\overline{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{Ni}^{2+} + \text{Cu}}$$

The electrode reaction in terms of reduction in anode is $\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Ni}$
$$E_{\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}} = E^0_{\text{Ni}^{2+}/\text{Ni}} - \frac{0.05912}{2} \log \frac{a_{\text{Ni}}}{a_{\text{Ni}^{2+}}} \quad [\text{since } a_{\text{Ni}} = 1]$$
$$= -0.15 + 0.0296 \log a_{\text{Ni}^{2+}} = -0.15 + 0.0296 \log 0.01 = -0.2092\text{ V}$$

The electrode reaction reduction occurs in cathode $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e \longrightarrow \text{Cu}$
$$E_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = E^0_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} - \frac{0.05912}{2} \log \frac{a_{\text{Cu}}}{a_{\text{Cu}^{2+}}}$$
$$= 0.34 + 0.0296 \log 0.1 \quad [\text{since } a_{\text{Cu}} = 1] = 0.3104\text{V}$$
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E_{\text{reduction}})_+ - (E_{\text{reduction}})_- = 0.3104 - (-0.2092) = 0.5196\text{ V.}$$

Thus free energy change $\Delta G = -nFE$
or, $-\Delta G = nFE = (2 \times 96500 \times 0.5196)\text{ J} = 100282.8\text{ J}$

Thus maximum work, $W_{\text{max}} = -(\Delta G)_T = 100282.8\text{ J}$

long.question: How does the equilibrium constant of a reversible chemical reaction is determined from standard EMF of the cell?
long.answer:
Let us consider a reversible chemical reaction
$$n_1 A + n_2 B + \cdots \rightleftharpoons n_3 D + n_4 E + \cdots$$

The equilibrium constant of this reaction is related to the standard free energy change of the process as
$$-RT \ln K_{(eq)} = \Delta G^0 \qquad \ldots(1)$$
But standard free energy change is related to the standard EMF of the cell as
$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 \qquad \ldots(2)$$
From (1) and (2) we have
$$-RT \ln K_{(eq)} = \Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$$
or, $\log K_{(eq)} = \dfrac{nFE^0}{RT \times 2.303}$
or, $K_{(eq)} = 10^{\frac{nFE^0}{RT \times 2.303}}$

So knowing $E^0$ and the temperature we can calculate the equilibrium constant.

long.question: How does the valency of an ion is determined from the EMF of a cell?
long.answer:
Let us construct an electrolyte concentration cell without transference such that the activity of the metal ion of the left hand electrode is 10 times greater than that of the right hand electrode.

The cell is given by $M \mid \overset{}{M^{+n}(a)} \parallel \overset{}{M^{+n}(a/10)} \mid M$

So EMF of the left hand electrode is
$$E_1 = E^0 - \frac{0.059}{n} \log a_1 \qquad \ldots(1)$$
So EMF of the right hand electrode is
$$E_2 = E^0 - \frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{a}{10} \qquad \ldots(2) \quad [\text{Where '}n\text{' is the valency of ion}]$$
Subtracting,
$$E_2 - E_1 = \frac{0.059}{n} \log \frac{a}{a/10} = \frac{0.059}{n} \log 10 = \frac{0.059}{n}$$
or, $E = \dfrac{0.059}{n}$
or, $n = \dfrac{0.059}{E}$ (at 25°C)

So measuring EMF of the cell we can measure the valency ($n$) of the metal at a definite temperature.

concepts.title: Hydrosphere
concepts.body:
The hydrosphere includes all types of water resources such as oceans, seas, rivers, lakes, streams, reservoirs, glacier and polar ice caps, and ground water.

- About **97%** of the earth's water supply is in the oceans, where the high salt content does not permit its use for human consumption.
- About **2%** of the water resource is locked in the glaciers and polar ice caps.
- Only **2%** is available as freshwater (surface water, rivers, lakes, streams and ground water) for human consumption and other uses.

The ground water is usually of good quality and thus can be used for drinking and for day-to-day use. The other sweet water often contains suspended matters, undesirable dissolved salts as well as micro-organisms which is harmful for health. This water cannot be used for drinking purpose. The major uses of water are for irrigation (30%) and thermal power plants (50%). Other uses are for domestic (7%) and industrial consumption (≈ 12%).

concepts.title: The hydrological cycle
concepts.body:
The hydrological cycle is a continuous natural process, which helps in exchange of water between the atmosphere, the land, the sea, the living plants and animals.

The hydrological cycle consists of five parts which provide the global water supply:

1. Massive evaporation of water from the oceans.
2. Cloud formation
3. Precipitation
4. Surface run-off and
5. Ground water movements.

concepts.title: Oxygen Demanding Wastes
concepts.body:
One of the most important measures of water quality is the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) present in it. The saturated value of DO in water is in the order of **8 – 15 mg/L**, depending on temperature and salinity. Optimum DO required for healthy fish and other aquatic life in natural water is **5 – 8 mg/L**, or as low as **3 mg/L** for less desirable species, such as corp.

The oxygen demanding wastes are substances that oxidize in the receiving body of water reducing the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) available in water.

**Origin:** Oxygen demanding wastes are usually biodegradable organic substances contained in municipal waste waters or effluents from certain industries, such as food processing and paper production. The oxidation of certain inorganic compounds may also contribute to the oxygen demanding wastes.

**Effects of drop of DO:**
1. If DO drops, fish and other aquatic life are threatened and in the extreme case, killed.
2. The effect of reduced DO is undesirable odors, tastes, and colours, reduce acceptability of water as a domestic supply and recreational purpose.

**Measurement of Oxygen Demand:**
1. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):** It is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the wastes.
2. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD):** It is the amount of oxygen required by microorganisms to biologically degrade the wastes.

concepts.title: Nutrients
concepts.body:
Nutrients are chemicals, such as nitrogen, phosphorus, carbon, sulphur, calcium, potassium, iron, manganese, boron and cobalt, that are essential for the growth of living things. The nutrients can be considered as pollutants when concentration are sufficient to allow excessive growth of aquatic plants, particularly algae.

**Effects:**
1. When nutrients stimulate the growth of algae, the attractiveness of water for recreational uses such as drinking water supply and as a viable habitat for other living things can be adversely affected.
2. Nutrient enrichment can lead to blooms of algae, which prevents the entry of sunlight into water bodies. Aquatic plants, along with algae thus die, the bacteria present in water now decompose all these dead plants.
3. Their decomposition removes oxygen from water which ultimately reduces the level of DO in water. The depletion of dissolved oxygen leads to the death of fishes, other aquatic animals and plants.
4. Algae and decaying organic matter add colour, odour, turbidity and objectionable taste to water that are difficult to remove and that may greatly reduce its acceptability as a domestic water source.

concepts.title: Limiting Nutrients
concepts.body:
Plants require relatively large amounts of each of three most important nutrients e.g. carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus which control the growth of algae blooms. The nutrient that is least available relative to the plants need is called the limited nutrient. Carbon is not the limited nutrient, as it is usually available from a number of natural sources like dissolved carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and decaying organic matter.

**Sources:**
1. Major sources of both nitrogen and phosphorus are municipal waste water discharges, run off from animal feed lots and chemical fertilizers.
2. The major unusual sources of nitrogen and phosphorus are soaps and detergents, chemical fertilizers like urea, ammonium sulphate or nitrate, super phosphate etc.
3. Certain bacteria and blue-green algae can obtain nitrogen directly from the atmosphere.
4. Certain forms of acid rain can also contribute nitrogen to lakes.

**Effect of Limiting Nutrients:**
1. When nitrogen is found in drinking water a particular form of it can pose a serious public health threat.
2. Nitrogen in water is commonly found in the form of nitrates ($\text{NO}_3^-$). Certain bacteria can convert nitrates ($\text{NO}_3^-$) to highly toxic nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$).
3. Haemoglobin has greater affinity for nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$) compared to oxygen, therefore it leads to replacement of oxygen by nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$), causing a bluish discolouration of the infant, which is commonly referred to as the **"blue baby" syndrome**.

concepts.title: Eutrophication
concepts.body:
Eutrophication is a process whereby water bodies, such as lakes or slow moving streams, receive excess nutrients that stimulate excessive plant growth. This enhanced plant growth, often called an algal bloom, reduces dissolved oxygen in the water when dead plant material decomposes and can cause other organisms to die. Nutrients can come from many sources, such as fertilizers applied to agricultural fields, golf courses, and suburban lawns; deposition of nitrogen from the atmosphere; erosion of soil containing nutrients and sewage treatment plant discharges. Water with a low concentration of dissolved oxygen is called hypoxic.

**Adverse effect of eutrophication:**
1. Increased biomass of phytoplankton.
2. Increases in blooms of gelatinous zooplankton.
3. Decreases in water transparency.
4. Taste, odour, and water treatment problems.
5. Dissolved oxygen depletion.
6. Increased incidences of fish kills.
7. Loss of desirable fish species.
8. Decreases in perceived aesthetic value of the water body.

concepts.title: Salts
concepts.body:
Water naturally accumulates a variety of dissolved solids or salts. Generally, the cations in the salt are $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{K}^+$ and the anions are $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ and $\text{HCO}_3^-$. More commonly used measure of salinity is the concentration of total dissolved solids (TDS).

- Fresh water contains 1500 mg/lit TDS.
- Brackish water may have TDS up to 5000 mg/lit.
- Saline water has TDS above 5000 mg/lit.
- Sea water contains 30,000–35,000 mg/lit TDS.

Drinking water has a recommended maximum TDS of **500 mg/lit** as per WHO's specification, but for Indian conditions, drinking water having TDS up to 1500 mg/lit has been permitted under certain conditions.

**Sources:**
- (a) Irrigation drainage water is always higher in salinity than the supply water and with every reuse, its salt concentration increases more.
- (b) Discharge of high concentration of salt from industries.
- (c) Removal of freshwater by evaporation, also causes salinity problems.

**Remedy:** The only approach for removal of salt from water is desalination. Different desalination technologies are available such as ion exchange resin, reverse osmosis and electrodialysis, but due to advancement of membrane technology the later two processes are energetically and economically favourable.

concepts.title: Hardness of water
concepts.body:
The presence of multivalent cations, specially calcium, magnesium, strontium, aluminium, ferrous and manganous ions are responsible for hardness of water.

**Effect of hard water:** When hard water is heated, calcium carbonate $[\text{CaCO}_3]$ and magnesium hydroxide $[\text{Mg(OH)}_2]$ readily precipitated out from the solution, forming scale which ultimately affect water pipes, reduces the efficiency of water heaters, boilers and heat exchangers.

**Types of hardness:** Hardness are classified into temporary hardness and permanent hardness.
- (i) **Temporary or carbonate hardness** is due to the dissolved salts of bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium and can be removed by boiling.
- (ii) **Permanent or non carbonate hardness** is mainly due to the dissolved salts of chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium.

**Expression of hardness:** Hardness of water is conveniently expressed in terms of parts of calcium carbonate hardness in million ($10^6$) parts of water (ppm).

Thus $X$ ppm of a compound contributing to hardness = $X$ parts of the compound present in $10^6$ parts of water.

$$= \frac{X \times 50}{E} \times \text{Parts of CaCO}_3 \text{ equivalents present in } 10^6 \text{ parts of water}$$

where $E$ = Equivalent weight of the compound.

concepts.title: Types of water treatment
concepts.body:
Water treatment for softening may be broadly classified into two types: (a) internal treatment, (b) external treatment.

**(a) Internal Treatment:** When water is used in boilers, the residual hardness can be eliminated by an internal treatment known as conditioning which means treatment of water within the boiler with chemicals which may capable of forming a sludge, easily removable by blow down operation or a water soluble co-ordination complex of very low in stability constant, with metal ions, regarded as scale formers. The technique of conditioning being strictly internal.

Different types of internal treatment are:
- (i) colloidal conditioning method
- (ii) phosphate conditioning method
- (iii) carbonate conditioning method
- (iv) Calgon conditioning method.

**(b) External treatment:** External treatment of hard water may be of three types:
- (i) Lime-soda process
- (ii) Zeolite process
- (iii) Ion exchange process.

---

long.question: What do you mean by hardness of water? Explain how hard water fails to form lather with soap? What are different types of hardness?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** The presence of multivalent cations, specially calcium, magnesium, strontium, aluminium, ferrous and manganous ions are responsible for hardness of water.

**2nd Part:** When soap is added to hard water, calcium and magnesium ions replaces sodium (or potassium) ions in the salt (soap) and gets precipitated hence lather formation is hindered.

**3rd Part:** Hardness are classified into temporary hardness and permanent hardness.
- (i) Temporary or carbonate hardness is due to the dissolved salts of bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium and can be removed by boiling.
- (ii) Permanent or non carbonate hardness is mainly due to the dissolved salts of chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium.

long.question: What is the reason for alkalinity in water?
long.answer:
The alkalinity of natural waters primarily due to the salts of weak acids, although weak or strong bases may contribute. Bicarbonates represent the major form of alkalinity, since they are formed in considerable amounts from the action of carbondioxide upon basic minerals in the soil as shown in the following reaction.

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$$

The bicarbonates along with carbonates and hydroxides which are responsible for the alkalinity of water can be explained by the following reactions.

**Alkalinity:**

1. Reaction (1): $\quad \text{CO}_2 + \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$

2. Reaction (2): $\quad \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{HCO}_3^-$$
$$\text{OH}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Other minor contributors to the alkalinity of water are salts of weak acids, such as borates, silicates and phosphates.

A few organic acids that is quite resistant to biological oxidation — for example humic acid — form salts that add alkalinity to the natural water. In polluted or anaerobic waters, salts of weak acids such as acetic, propionic and hydrosulphuric may be produced and would also contribute to alkalinity. In other cases, ammonia or hydroxides may make a contribution to the total alkalinity of water.

The alkalinity of water is mainly due to salts of weak acids and strong bases and these substances act as buffers to resist a drop in pH resulting from the acid addition. Alkalinity is thus a measure of buffer capacity and in this sense is used to a great extent in waste water treatment practice.

long.question: What is the reason for acidity in water?
long.answer:
The acidity due to dissolved $\text{CO}_2$ is called $\text{CO}_2$ acidity. Mineral acidity is present in many industrial wastes, particularly those of metallurgical industry and some production of synthetic organic materials. Certain natural water may also contain mineral acidity. The drainage from abandoned mines will contain significant amounts of sulphuric acid or salt of sulphuric acid if sulphur, sulphides or iron pyrites are present. Conversion of these materials to $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and sulphates is facilitated by sulphur oxidizing bacteria under aerobic conditions.

The acidic character of some hydrated metal ion may contribute to acidity as shown in the following reactions.

**Acidity:**

$$2\text{S} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$\text{FeS}_2 + 3\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

Salts of heavy metals, particularly those of trivalent metal ions such as $\text{Fe}^{+3}$ and $\text{Al}^{+3}$ hydrolyse in water to release mineral acidity.

$$\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 + 3\text{H}^+ + 3\text{Cl}^-$$

long.question: What is hardness of water? What are the different classifications of hardness of water? What are the effects of hardness of water?
long.answer:
The presence of multivalent cations, specially calcium, magnesium, strontium, aluminium, ferrous and manganous ions are responsible for hardness of water.

Hardness is classified into temporary hardness and permanent hardness.
- (i) Temporary or carbonate hardness is due to the dissolved salts of bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium and can be removed by boiling.
- (ii) Permanent or non carbonate hardness is mainly due to the dissolved salts of chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium.

When hard water is heated, calcium carbonate $[\text{CaCO}_3]$ and magnesium hydroxide $[\text{Mg(OH)}_2]$ readily precipitated out from the solution, forming scale which ultimately affect water pipes, reduces the efficiency of water heaters, boilers and heat exchangers.

long.question: How does carbonate and non carbonate hardness can be removed by lime soda process? What are the advantages and disadvantages of lime soda process?
long.answer:
Either quick lime ($\text{CaO}$) or hydrated lime ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$) is added to the water, raising the pH to about 10.3, and converting soluble $\text{HCO}_3^-$ ions into insoluble $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ which precipitate out as $\text{CaCO}_3$.

**Carbonate:**

$$\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

For removal of non carbonate hardness the reagent is a mixture of quick lime or slacked lime and 98% $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, the lime being taken in slight excess.

**Non-Carbonate:**

$$\text{CaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}$$
$$\text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$\text{MgCO}_3 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow$$
$$\text{MgCl}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{CaCl}_2$$
$$\text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4 \downarrow$$

**Advantages:**
- (i) The process is very economical.
- (ii) Treated water is alkaline and hence has less corrosion tendencies.
- (iii) It removes not only hardness causing salts but also minerals.
- (iv) Due to alkaline nature of treated water, amount of pathogenic bacterias in water is considerably reduced.
- (v) Iron and manganese are also removed from the water to some extent.

**Disadvantages:**
- (i) It requires careful operation and skilled supervision for economical and efficient softening.
- (ii) Sludge disposal is a problem.
- (iii) Water softened by this method contains appreciable concentrations of soluble salts, such as sodium sulphate and cannot be used in high pressure boilers.

long.question: What is permutit or zeolite process? Which materials are used in the zeolite bed? How does the hardness bearing salts are removed in zeolite process? How will you regenerate zeolite bed? What are the advantages and disadvantages of zeolite process?
long.answer:
Permutits or zeolites are a class of naturally occurring or synthetically prepared base exchanging silicates capable of removing both types of hardness by exchanging sodium of the zeolite with $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ of water in equivalent amounts.

Two naturally occurring zeolites, natrolite ($\text{Na}_2\text{O}, \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3, 3\text{SiO}_2, 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$) and analcine ($\text{Na}_2\text{O}, \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3, 4\text{SiO}_2, 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$) are the most common variety in use for the purpose.

For softening of water by zeolite process hard water is percolated at a specified rate through a bed of zeolite, housed in a cylindrical unit. Zeolite holds sodium ions loosely and can be simply represented as $\text{Na}_2\text{Ze}$ where Ze represents zeolite. The hardness causing ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ etc.) are retained by the zeolite as $\text{CaZe}$ and $\text{MgZe}$ respectively, while the outgoing water contains sodium salts. In the process, the water becomes free from $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$.

The softening reactions may be represented as follows:

$$\text{Na}_2\text{Ze} + \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{CaZe} + 2\text{NaHCO}_3$$
$$\text{Na}_2\text{Ze} + \text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{MgZe} + 2\text{NaHCO}_3$$
$$\text{Na}_2\text{Ze} + \text{CaCl}_2 \text{ or (CaSO}_4) \rightarrow \text{CaZe} + 2\text{NaCl (or Na}_2\text{SO}_4)$$

After sometimes the zeolite is completely converted into calcium and magnesium zeolites. The exhausted zeolite is converted into sodium zeolite by washing with a weak solution of sodium chloride.

**Regeneration:**

$$\text{CaZe (or MgZe)} + 2\text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{Ze} + \text{CaCl}_2 \text{ (or MgCl}_2)$$

**Advantages of zeolite process:**
- (i) Hardness is nearly completely removed and water of about 10 ppm hardness is produced.
- (ii) The equipment used is compact and occupies less space.
- (iii) It is quite clean and rapid process.
- (iv) Less requirement of skilled maintenance.
- (v) Allows wide range of temperature fluctuations.
- (vi) No sludge formation.
- (vii) Simultaneous removal of Fe (ous) impurities.
- (viii) Operation expenses are lower than that of the lime soda process.

**Disadvantages:**
- (i) If the supplied water is turbid, the suspended matter must be removed before the water is fed to the zeolite bed.
- (ii) Mineral acids present in water will destroy the zeolite bed and the mineral acid must be neutralized by soda.
- (iii) If water contains large quantities of $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ and $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, they must be removed first because these ions produce manganese and iron zeolite which are very difficult to be regenerated.
- (iv) There is always a risk of disintegration of the bed with consequent loss of activity.
- (v) Lead does not seem to be completely exchanged by the technique.
- (vi) Anions are not removed by the process.

long.question: Which one is the most efficient process for removal of hardness of water? Discuss the process with suitable advantages and disadvantages.
long.answer:
The most efficient process for removal of hardness of water is ion exchange process.

**Process:** In the demineralisation process, the raw water is first forced through a bed of cation exchanger where the metal ions in water are replaced by hydrogen ions and the water become distinctly acidic. It next passes through an anion exchanger column where the anions are replaced by $\text{OH}^-$ in amounts equivalent to $\text{H}^+$, already formed in the cation exchanger, so that the effluent leaving the anion exchanger is exactly neutral with no trace of mineral impurities. It passes next a degasification unit to be free from dissolved $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$ and then stored.

**Cation exchange resin:** They are mainly sulphonated styrene resins, sulphonated phenol formaldehyde resins or carboxylic acid resin. Resins of this type can exchange all cations from water through hydrogen exchange as a result of which water becomes distinctly acidic after treatment.

$$\text{M(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\text{RSO}_3\text{H} \rightarrow (\text{RSO}_3)_2\text{M} + 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{MSO}_4 + 2\text{RSO}_3\text{H} \rightarrow (\text{RSO}_3)_2\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$\text{MCl}_2 + 2\text{RSO}_3\text{H} \rightarrow (\text{RSO}_3)_2\text{M} + 2\text{HCl}$$

**Anion exchange resin:** They are polyamine formaldehyde resin, styrene-divinyl benzene copolymer, which contain basic functional groups such as amine, quarternary ammonium or quarternary phosphonium or tertiary sulphonium groups as an integral part of resin matrix. These after treatment with dilute $\text{NaOH}$ solution, become capable of exchange in their $\text{OH}^-$ anions in water and therefore they are known as anion exchange resin.

$$[\text{M} = \text{Ca, Mg, Fe}]$$
$$\text{RNH}_2 + \text{HOH} \rightarrow \text{RN}\overset{(+)}{\text{H}}_3\,\overset{(-)}{\text{OH}}$$
$$\text{RN}\overset{(+)}{\text{H}}_3\,\overset{(-)}{\text{OH}} + \text{HX} \rightarrow \text{RN}\overset{(+)}{\text{H}}_3\,\overset{(-)}{\text{X}} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$(\text{X} = \text{Cl}^-, \text{HSO}_4^- \text{ etc})$$

**Regeneration — Cation exchange resins:** Regeneration of the cation active bed is carried out by reversing the exchange reaction.

$$(\text{RSO}_3)_2\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (excess)} \rightarrow \text{MSO}_4 + 2\text{RSO}_3\text{H}$$
$$2\text{RSO}_3\text{Na} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (excess)} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{RSO}_3\text{M}$$

**Regeneration — Anion exchange resins:** Regeneration of the anion exchange bed is carried out by a strong $\text{NaOH}$ solution.

**Advantages:**
- (i) Water obtained is of extra pure quality.
- (ii) No extra power requirement and no heating.
- (iii) Long service of the resin bed due to high mechanical and chemical stability.
- (iv) The process can be used to soften highly acidic or alkaline waters.
- (v) Sugar juices with mineral constituents can be processed on such beds.
- (vi) It produces water of very low hardness.
- (vii) Formaldehyde contaminated with formic acid can be purified on an anion exchanger.

**Disadvantages:**
- (i) Capital cost is high since chemical and equipment both are costly.
- (ii) If water contains turbidity then the efficiency of the process is reduced.

long.question: Describe hydrological cycle.
long.answer:
The hydrological cycle is a continuous natural process, which helps in exchange of water among the atmosphere, the land, the sea, the living plants and animals.

It consists of five steps which maintain the global water supply:
- a) Massive evaporation of water
- b) Cloud formation
- c) Precipitation
- d) Surface run-off and
- e) Ground water movements.

About 66% of the solar flux absorbed by the earth is used to drive the hydrological cycle i.e., massive evaporation of water from the ocean, seas, river, lakes, ponds etc occurs. The water vapours in the atmosphere form clouds and are transported through moving air masses. When the clouds meet with the cold air, condense and precipitated as rain due to gravitational pull.

Water as rain or snow is precipitated on all land and water surfaces. Water on land surface reaches to the ground water through the soil by the impervious layer of rock and clay.

Below ground there is a natural water level or water table. Water moves up above the table by capillary action and thereby maintain a continuous supply of water to the surface layer of soil, where it is absorbed by plant roots in absence of rain.

long.question: What is oxygen demanding wastes in water body? What is the saturated value of dissolved oxygen and the optimum value of dissolved oxygen in the water body? What are the origin of oxygen demanding wastes in water body?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** The oxygen demanding wastes are substances that oxidize in the receiving body of water reducing the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) available in water.

**2nd Part:** The saturated value of dissolved oxygen (DO) is in the order of 8–15 mg/L. The optimum dissolved oxygen (DO) required for healthy fish and aquatic life in natural water is 5–8 mg/L.

**3rd Part:** Oxygen demanding wastes are generally biodegradable organic substances contained in municipal waste waters or effluent from certain industries, like food processing and paper production. The oxidation of certain inorganic compounds may also contribute to the oxygen demanding wastes.

long.question: What are the effects of drop of dissolved oxygen in the water body? Which methods are employed to measure the dissolved oxygen in the water body?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** Following things may occur if the dissolved oxygen content in the water body is decreased:
- a) If DO drops, fish and other aquatic life are threatened and in the extreme case, killed.
- b) The effect of reduced DO is responsible for undesirable odors, tastes and colors which ultimately reduce the acceptability of water as a domestic supply and recreational purpose.

**2nd Part:** Following two methods are employed to measure the DO content in the water body:
- a) Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)
- b) Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

long.question: What is dissolved oxygen? What is its importance as water quality parameter?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** Available oxygen present in dissolved condition in water in known as dissolved oxygen. It is important water quality parameters. The saturated value of dissolved oxygen (DO) in water is in the order of 8–15 mg/L depending on temperature and salinity.

**2nd Part:** DO is very important water quality parameter because:
- i) The BOD test, which measures the amount of biodegradable wastes present in water, is based on DO.
- ii) The quality of waste water is determined on the basis of DO.
- iii) Oxygen is responsible for corrosion. Hence determination of DO helps in controlling corrosion.

long.question: "Excessive nutrient concentration in water is responsible for water pollution." — Explain.
long.answer:
*OR,* **What are the effects of excessive nutrient concentration in water?** *[MODEL QUESTION]*

Nutrients are chemicals, such as nitrogen, phosphorous, carbon, sulphur, calcium, potassium, iron, manganese, boron and cobalt, that are essential for the growth of living things. The nutrient can be considered as pollutants when the concentrations are sufficient and as a result following undesirable circumstances may develop.

- i) Nutrient enrichment can lead to blooms of algae, which prevents the entry of sunlight into water bodies. Therefore, the aquatic plants along with algae are died; the bacteria present in water now decompose all these dead plants.
- ii) Their decomposition removes oxygen from water which ultimately reduces the level of DO in water. The depletion of dissolved oxygen leads to the death of fishes, other aquatic animals or plants.
- iii) When nutrients simulate the growth of algae, the attractiveness of water for recreational uses such as drinking water supply and as a viable habitat for other living things can be adversely affected.
- iv) Algae and decaying organic matter add colour, odour, turbidity and objectionable taste to water that are difficult to remove and that may greatly reduce its acceptability as a domestic water resource.

long.question: What is limiting nutrients? What are the sources of limiting nutrient in water? What are the effects of limiting nutrient in water?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** The nutrient that is least available relative to the plants need is called the limiting nutrient. Carbon, nitrogen and phosphorous are limiting nutrients because plants require relatively large amounts of each of these three nutrients for their growth.

**2nd Part:** Sources of limiting nutrients:
- i) Carbon is not the limiting nutrient, as it is usually available from a number of natural sources such as, dissolved carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and decaying organic matter.
- ii) Major sources of both nitrogen and phosphorous are municipal waste water discharges, run off from animal feed lots and chemical fertilizers.
- iii) The major unusual sources of nitrogen and phosphorous are soaps and detergents, chemical fertilizers like urea, ammonium sulphate or nitrate, super-phosphate etc.
- iv) Certain forms of acid rain can also contribute nitrogen to lakes.

**3rd Part:** Effect of limiting nutrients:
- i) Where nitrogen is found in drinking water a particular form of it can pose a serious public health threat.
- ii) Nitrogen in water is commonly found in the form of nitrates ($\text{NO}_3^-$), certain bacteria can convert nitrates ($\text{NO}_3^-$) to highly toxic nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$).
- iii) Haemoglobin has greater affinity to nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$) than that of the oxygen, therefore it leads to replacement of oxygen by nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$), causing a bluish discolouration of the infant, which is commonly known as 'blue baby' syndrome.

long.question: Which salts are responsible for TDS in water? What is recommended maximum TDS of drinking water as per WHO's specification? What are the sources of dissolved solids or salts in water? How the TDS from water can be removed?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** Water naturally accumulates variety of dissolved solids or salt. The cation in the salt are $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{K}^+$ and the anions are $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ and $\text{HCO}_3^-$. More commonly used measure of salinity is the concentration of total dissolved solids (TDS).

**2nd Part:** Drinking water has a recommended maximum TDS of 500 mg/L as per WHO's specification.

**3rd Part:**
- i) Irrigation drainage water is always higher in salinity than the supply water and with every reuse, its salt concentration increases more.
- ii) Discharge of high concentration of salt from industries.
- iii) Removal of fresh water by evaporation also causes salinity problem.

**4th Part:** The only approach for removal of salt from water is desalination. The salt is removed from water by electro-dialysis process.

**Electro-dialysis:** In this process ion selective membranes and an electrical potential difference is used to separate the cations and the anions from the solution.

[Diagram: A simple dialysis (electro-dialysis) cell. Anionic and cationic membranes are arranged alternately between two electrodes to form many compartments. Membranes passing only "−" ions and only "+" ions are shown; the cell is fed with salt solution and yields deionised water. [diagram]]

The figure shows a simple dialysis cell in which waste water may be deionised. From the figure it is evident that two types of membranes (anionic and cationic) are arranged alternatively to form many compartments between the electrodes placed at the two ends. When the voltage is applied across the cells containing mineralised water, the anion migrates to the positive electrode and the cation migrates to the negative electrode. As a result solution in alternate compartments becomes more concentrated while that in the remaining becomes more dilute. The electric power requirement is proportional to the number of ions removed from the water.

long.question: How the TDS from water can be removed by reverse osmosis process?
long.answer:
In reverse osmosis process, demineralised water is produced by forcing water through semi-permeable membranes.

In ordinary osmosis, if a vessel is divided by a semi-permeable membrane, and one compartment is filled with water and other with concentrated salt solution, water diffused through the membrane towards the compartment containing salt solution until the difference in water levels on the two sides of the membrane creates a sufficient pressure to counteract the original water flow.

- The difference in levels represents the osmotic pressure of the solution.
- The process can be reversed by applying sufficient pressure called hydrostatic pressure to the concentrated solution to overcome osmotic pressure, the flow of solvent reverses as it is forced to move from concentrated side to the dilute side across the membrane.
- The solute concentration builds up in one side of the membrane while relatively pure water passing through the membrane.
- In order to obtain adequate solvent flux through the membrane, pressure of the order of 4000–7000 kN/m² is required.
- Membranes are cellulose acetate, PMMA and etc.

long.question: What is biochemical oxygen demand? How will you determine the biochemical oxygen demand of water?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** Biochemical oxygen demand is the amount of oxygen required by micro-organisms to biologically degrade the waste.

The aerobic decomposition of wastes present in water by micro-organisms produces non-objectionable, stable and products such as $\text{CO}_2$, sulphate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$), orthophosphate ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}$) and nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$).

$$\text{Organic matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microorganisms}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New cells} + \text{Stable products}$$

BOD is often expressed in milligram/litre.

**2nd Part:** The BOD of water is determined through 5-day BOD test. The 5 days BOD test or $\text{BOD}_5$ is the total amount of oxygen consumed by micro-organisms during the first 5-day of biodegradation.

- The test is performed over a 5 day period at 20°C in 300 mL bottles incubated in the dark to prevent interference from algal growth.
- A sample of waste water is taken in a stoppered bottle.
- The concentration of dissolved oxygen is determined at the beginning of the test.
- The concentration of dissolved oxygen is determined after 5-day period.

The difference is dissolved oxygen would be the 5-days BOD. It is usually necessary to dilute the sample to keep final dissolved oxygen above zero.

The 5 days BOD of a diluted sample is given by

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{\text{DO}_i - \text{DO}_f}{P}$$

where $\text{DO}_i$ = the initial dissolved oxygen of the diluted waste water; $\text{DO}_f$ = the final dissolved oxygen of the diluted waste water.

$$P = \text{the diluted fraction} = \frac{\text{Volume of waste water}}{\text{Volume of waste water} + \text{Dilution water}}$$

As standard BOD bottle holds 300 mL, so $P$ is just the volume of waste water divided by 300 mL.

long.question: Describe seeded BOD test and derive the formula:
long.answer:
$$\text{BOD}_w = \frac{(\text{DO}_i - \text{DO}_f) - (B_i - B_f)(1 - P)}{P}$$
*[MODEL QUESTION]*

In some cases it is necessary to seed the dilution water with micro-organisms to assure there is an adequate bacterial population to carry out biodegradation.

- Two bottles will be prepared, one containing just the seeded dilution water (blank) and the other containing the mixture of both the waste water and seeded dilution water.

The oxygen demand of the waste ($\text{BOD}_a$) can there be determined as:

$$\text{BOD}_m V_m = \text{BOD}_w \times V_w + \text{BOD}_d V_d \qquad \ldots(1)$$

where, $\text{BOD}_m$ = BOD of the mixture of waste water and seeded dilution water; $\text{BOD}_w$ = BOD of waste water alone; $\text{BOD}_d$ = BOD of seeded dilution water alone; $V_w$ = The volume of waste water in the mixture; $V_d$ = The volume of dilution water in the mixture; $V_m$ = The volume of the mixture $= V_d + V_w$.

Let, $P$ = Fraction of waste water in the mixture $= \dfrac{V_w}{V_m}$.

Thus, $1 - P$ = Fraction of seeded dilution water in the mixture $= \dfrac{V_d}{V_m}$.

Thus from equation (1),

$$\text{BOD}_w = \text{BOD}_m \times \frac{V_m}{V_w} - \text{BOD}_d \times \frac{V_d}{V_w}$$

$$= \text{BOD}_m \times \frac{V_m}{V_w} - \text{BOD}_d \left[\frac{V_d}{V_m} \times \frac{V_m}{V_w}\right] = \text{BOD}_m \times \frac{V_m}{V_w} - \text{BOD}_d \times \frac{\frac{V_d}{V_m}}{\frac{V_w}{V_m}}$$

$$= \frac{\text{BOD}_m}{P} - \text{BOD}_d \times \frac{1 - P}{P} = \frac{\text{BOD}_m - \text{BOD}_d(1 - P)}{P}$$

Here, $\text{BOD}_m = \text{DO}_i - \text{DO}_f$ and $\text{BOD}_d = B_i - B_f$,

where $\text{DO}_i$ = Initial dissolved oxygen of the mixture of waste water and seeded dilution water; $\text{DO}_f$ = Final dissolved oxygen of the mixture of waste water and seeded dilution water after 5-day period; $B_i$ = Initial dissolved oxygen in the seeded dilution water (Blank); $B_f$ = Final dissolved oxygen in the seeded dilution water (Blank).

long.question: Prove that
long.answer:
$\text{BOD}_t = C_a\left(1 - e^{-Kt}\right)$**.** *[MODEL QUESTION]*

$\text{BOD}_t$ = Biochemical oxygen demand after time $t$.
$C_a$ = The total amount of $\text{O}_2$ required by the micro-organisms to oxidize the waste.
$K$ = BOD reaction rate constant.

The aerobic decomposition of wastes present in the water by micro-organisms may be represented as:

$$\text{Organic matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microorganisms}} \text{Product}$$

Assuming first order reaction

$$-\frac{dc}{dt} \propto C$$
$$\text{or,} \quad -\frac{dc}{dt} = KC \qquad \ldots(1)$$

$-\dfrac{dc}{dt}$ is the rate of change of concentration of $\text{O}_2$. $C$ is the concentration of oxygen at any time $t$, $K$ is a constant i.e., velocity constant of BOD reaction rate ($\text{time}^{-1}$). If we assume $C_a$ is the initial value of dissolved oxygen, then from equation (1)

$$-\frac{dc}{C} = K\,dt$$

On integration $-\displaystyle\int \frac{dc}{C} = K \int dt$

$$\text{or,} \quad -\ln C = Kt + Z \qquad \ldots(2), \ (\text{Integration constant})$$

At time $t = 0$, $C = C_a$, then from equation (2), we get $Z = -\ln C_a$.

Hence, $-\ln C = Kt - \ln C_a$

$$\text{or,} \quad \ln \frac{C}{C_a} = -Kt$$
$$\text{or,} \quad C = C_a e^{-Kt}$$
$$(C_a - C) = C_a\left(1 - e^{-Kt}\right)$$

Since $C_a$ is the total amount of $\text{O}_2$ required by the micro-organisms to oxidize the organic waste, $C$ is the amount of $\text{O}_2$ consumed after time $t$. $(C_a - C)$ is the biochemical oxygen demand after time $t$.

$$(C_a - C) = \text{BOD}_t = C_a\left(1 - e^{-Kt}\right).$$

long.question: Which factors influence the amount of dissolved oxygen in water?
long.answer:
Following factors affect the amount of dissolved oxygen in water:
- a) Oxygen demanding wastes remove dissolved oxygen.
- b) Plants add dissolved oxygen during the day but remove it at night.
- c) The respiration of living organisms in sediments removes oxygen.
- d) In summer, rising temperatures reduce the solubility of oxygen, which ultimately decrease the rate at which oxygen enters the water from the atmosphere.

long.question: What is chemical oxygen demand? Why $\text{KMnO}_4$ is not used for determination of COD of water? How will you determine the COD of water?
long.answer:
**1st Part:** Chemical oxygen demand is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the waste present in water.

In COD test the organic compounds present in the water can be fully oxidized to carbon dioxide, water and ammonia.

$$\text{Organic compounds} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3$$

The expression does not include the oxygen required due to oxidation of ammonia to nitrate.

$$\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + \text{H}_3\text{O}^+$$

The process by which ammonia is converted to nitrate is known as nitrification.

**2nd Part:** Previously, the strong oxidizing agent potassium permanganate ($\text{KMnO}_4$) was used for measurement of chemical oxygen demand. Potassium permanganate's effectiveness for oxidizing organic compounds varies widely, and in many cases it is formed that the values for BOD is much greater than that of the values of COD measurement which indicates that potassium permanganate was not able to effectively oxidize all organic compounds in water. Therefore, potassium permanganate is not used as oxidizing agent for COD measurement.

**3rd Part:** To determine COD, a fixed volume with known excess of strong oxidizing agent, $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ in 50% sulphuric acid in presence of $\text{Ag}_2\text{SO}_4$ catalyst and $\text{HgSO}_4$ (to eliminate interference due to chloride) is added to the sample solution being analysed.

- The resultant mixture is refluxed to oxidize the organic substances present in water.
- Once oxidation is complete the amount of excess potassium dichromate will be determined through the titration with ferrous ammonium sulphate solution, until the entire excess oxidizing agent has been reduced to $\text{Cr}^{+3}$.
- The titration is carried out in presence of ferrous indicator. Once all of the excess dichromate has been reduced, the ferrous indicator changes from blue-green to reddish green.
- A blank titration is carried out to eliminate the accidental presence of organic substances in distilled water.

The amount of ferrous ammonium sulphate added is equivalent to the amount of excess $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ added to the original sample.

$$\text{Thus, COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{S}$$

where $V_1$ is the initial volume of ferrous ammonium sulphate solution of normally $N$ required for blank and $V_2$ for remaining untreated dichromate solution, $S$ is the volume of solution taken for testing.

---

*End of Chapter 7: Water Chemistry.*

concepts.title: Types of corrosion
concepts.body:
The process of corrosion of metals depends on the environment in which the metals are exposed. It may be divided into two types: (a) dry corrosion and (b) wet corrosion.

**Dry corrosion:** When a metal is exposed to a dry environment, the corrosion of metal occurs due to the direct attack of $\text{O}_2$ or corrosive gases such as $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{SO}_2$, or chemical action on metals by chemical reactions.

The possible reasons for dry corrosion are:
- a) Oxidation corrosion
- b) Corrosion by gases
- c) Liquid metal corrosion

concepts.title: Oxidation corrosion
concepts.body:
In this type of corrosion, metals are attacked by dry oxygen under extreme conditions such as low or high temperature, leading to oxidation type of corrosion.

The alkali and alkaline earth metals are prone to oxidize at low temperature, but metals like Fe, Al etc. are oxidized at high temperature in presence of oxygen.

The following reaction takes place during oxidation of metals:

$$\text{Mg} \longrightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e \quad \text{(oxidation)}$$
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2e \longrightarrow \text{O}^{2-} \quad \text{(reduction)}$$
$$\text{Mg} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{O}^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{MgO}$$

concepts.title: Corrosion by gases
concepts.body: Certain gases like $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{F}_2$ attack the metal in the absence of moisture, and the extent of corrosion depends on the formation of a thin film which may be protective or non-protective on the surface of the metal.

concepts.title: Liquid metal corrosion
concepts.body: Liquid metal corrosion is caused due to chemical reaction of solid metal with a flowing liquid metal at high temperature. Such kind of corrosion occurs in nuclear power production.

concepts.title: Mechanism of electrochemical corrosion
concepts.body:
The absorption of oxygen type corrosion occurs during rusting of iron.

During rusting at the anodic area of the metal, iron dissolves as $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e$ (oxidation). The corrosion occurs in the anodic area.

The liberated electrons move from the anodic area to the cathodic area where reduction reaction occurs with the absorption of oxygen and forms $\text{OH}^-$ ions.

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e \longrightarrow 2\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(reduction)}$$

Thus $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$ is precipitated on the surface of the metal.

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \downarrow$$

concepts.title: Pilling–Bedworth rule
concepts.body:
The protective or non-protective oxide layer formation in metals is governed by a rule known as the Pilling–Bedworth rule.

The rule states that "If the volume of the oxide is equal to or greater than the volume of the metal from which it is formed, the oxide layer is non-porous and therefore protective in nature (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$), whereas if the volume of the oxide is less than the volume of the metal from which it is formed, the oxide layer is porous and therefore non-protective in nature (e.g., alkali or alkaline metal oxides)."

concepts.title: Factors influencing the rate of corrosion
concepts.body:
- **Nature of metals:** Metals with low reduction potential exhibit high reactivity, and hence are more prone to corrosion.
- **Electrode potential differences:** Potential difference between the anodic and cathode area is known as open circuit potential difference. Larger the open circuit potential difference, higher the rate of corrosion.
- **Surface state of the metal:** State of the surface of the metal affects the rate of corrosion. If part of the surface is covered by oil or dust particles, severe pitting corrosion occurs at the metal surface.
- **Hydrogen over voltages:** A metal with low hydrogen over voltage on its surface is more susceptible to corrosion. Low hydrogen over voltage results in faster cathodic reaction.
- **Formation of protective films by metals:** A few metals have the tendency to form passive but protective films on exposure to corrosive medium. The nature of such passive layer greatly decides the rate of corrosion.
- **pH:** In general, lower the pH of the corrosion medium, higher the corrosion rate. At pH > 10, corrosion of Fe ceases due to the formation of a protective coating of $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$. Al, Zn undergo fast corrosion in highly alkaline solution. At pH < 3, severe corrosion occurs even in the absence of air due to continuous evolution of $\text{H}_2$ gas at the cathodic area.
- **Temperature:** Increase in temperature increases the conductance of the medium, reduces passivity of the metal, and therefore increases the rate of corrosion.
- **Cathodic and anodic area:** The relative size of cathodic and anodic area greatly affects the rate of corrosion.

---
